lines.st: He leaves the bed of withered flowers.
They say it is a sign of a bountiful harvest.
And so some walked in the embrace of night,
Some shared nectar before the bonfire,
And some walked hand in hand,
Lost together where the story diverged.
Shut the lips that sound the horn, he said.
Close the eyes about to witness eternity, he said.
To pick the fruit of pain.
He leaves the bed of withered flowers.
He writes his epitaph into the stirring dust,

lines.x: Does Lady Dupont still shut herself in her room?
It's been three days, and she's hardly touched her food. What shall we do?
The way I see it, the madam is fretting over nothing.
As heir to the Dupont family, Victoria's most noble lineage, she is both elegant and intelligent, and no one could fault her for choosing any of her three suitors.
But that's exactly why she's so troubled.
The three Rockefeller brothers may look like they come from the same handsome mold, but they each have wildly different personalities and occupations.
When a wise scholar, a brave soldier, and a romantic poet all ask for your hand at once, can you even imagine choosing which to spend your life with?
Besides, whatever choice she makes is bound to bring great pain to the other two.
Why, the very thought!
The very thought of the bonuses we'll get when one of those millionaires moves in! Whatever choice she makes, I hope she chooses soon!
How could you be so—
Cut!
Director Quay?
Stiff as damned statues.
(Mutters) Well, it's just exposition anyway. Might as well cut the scene and add a few symbolic empty shots later...
(Mutters) Let's just hope our overzealous producer lady doesn't sweat the small stuff.
You two take a break while we make some adjustments to the script... Actually, don't bother coming back for shooting.
More adjustments? This guy just can't make up his mind.
Shh—
Alright, people, we're not taking a break just yet. So shut up already... shut up!

Yes yes, I know we've been shooting for two months, and everyone's tired, and the plate's far from the city center, and there are no casinos or shopping districts here, and everything at the self-service bar is undrinkable pisswater...
BUT! For better or worse, this is our last week. Time is precious and so is my film.
The Death of Lady Dupont is the kind of historical tragedy they don't make in Wrankwood these days, exploring Columbia's changing times through the lens of a noblewoman's marriage...
So I don't want it looking like some cheap cash-grab when it finally hits theaters!
Let's move on to the next run-through.
The Death of Lady Dupont, balcony, scene three... Melanie and Michael, you're up.
What's the matter, Miss Rutherford?
What do you think, boy?
Oh, I'm sorry. I shouldn't have made a veteran like you wait a whole minute for me to get ready.
Do you think I'm making a big deal out of nothing?
Of course not. Alongside Marianne Blake, you used to be one of the greatest actresses in Wrankwood.
Seniority, status, etiquette... Hate them as much as you like deep down, but you will respect them on my set.
I'm sorry if I offended you, ma'am.
So are we ready?
I know it's not easy playing three different characters, but don't mix them up like you did last time. You're playing the SECOND Rockefeller, the soldier.

You're at Lady Dupont's manor, and have just thrown away your older brother's flowers and torn up your younger brother's love letter. You're rushing into your beloved's bedroom to pour your heart out!
Silence, please—Action!
Believe me, my love, my two brothers could never match my feelings for you!
I've heard more than enough honeyed words, Mr. Rockefeller.
Were I not the scion of Dupont, were our union to bring the Rockefellers no wealth or status in high society, would you still—
You wound me. I am no schemer, and I mean every word I say.
You know I'm not as smart or sensitive as my brothers. How can I hope to move you, if not through the sincerity of my feelings?
And how will you prove it?
I recently returned from the battlefield, where I brushed shoulders with death.
When that shell burst right next to me, when I felt my body and my senses flying away, when I came close to death's door...
All I could picture was your face, your smile!
Er... you have the next line, ma'am—
Despicable.
Uh—What?
What kind of soldier would twist his face up like you did when speaking of the battlefield? You put so much honey in your words, you could stick a fly to the ground with it.
I know you've spent the last two months making moves on anything even vaguely female on this plate, but for God's sake keep your private life off the set, boy.
Not everyone will fall for a wiggle of your ears and a swoosh of your orange tail like that dumb blonde.
D-Dumb blonde...?
M-Marion! What are you doing here?
Why wouldn't the costume designer be on set for the run-through? I need to see if the costumes are in order.
Or what? Don't tell me you also believe that gossip the rest of the crew's been spreading?
Right, of course. There's no problem.
Moving on... Michael, your performance needs to fit the character better, or it won't come across as sincere.
The second Rockefeller's sincerity is the whole point. Without it, how can we possibly convince the audience that this is what pushes Lady Dupont to finally make her decision?
You two can take a break while we move on to the next one.
Abner?
I'm here.
We need an evocative transition into the next scene.
Gotcha. We'll go with the plan we talked about yesterday.
Also, can you stay away from that metal rack? I'm worried the screws will scratch the camera.
I can't tell if you're the winner of Wrankwood's Best Cinematography award, or the least professional cameraman in town.
Why not both?
What happened to you, man?
No need to make a fuss of it.
As Lady Dupont and the second Rockefeller's conversation fades out, the camera pans away from the balcony...
It moves down the steps, across a row of portraits and antique vases, to the front hall draped with red curtains.
The front door opens.
The sound of wind and rain trespasses into the quiet manor. The camera follows, and a black shadow enters the frame—
Mrrow~
What the hell's that black shadow?!
Is it a ghost? A prop? A hallucination? Somebody's Friedkin pet?
Do none of you idiots realize what kind of havoc an animal like this could wreak on a film set?!
Grab it, Abner! Don't let it mess with the camera rails on the ground!
With what hands? Or do you want me to smash the damned camera?
It went your way, Marion.
I don't have any free hands either! And are you prepared to take responsibility if the costumes get wrinkled? The producer would have a fit!
Someone stop it!
Mrow—

The mysterious and elegant creature darts through the set, weaving between the wires and delicate equipment laid out over the ground, its two tails moving this way and that as cries are heard up and down the crowd...
Stop painting that background and catch that thing, Tippi! And you there, the prop intern—
Her name is Moira. Moi-ra.
Forget it, Tippi.
You're, like, way too nice, Molly. You can't let people walk all over you.
Who cares? Just catch that thing!
I-It stopped.
Well, aren't you a cutie! You're even more mysterious than the monster in that horror flick I saw last night!
Mind if I use you as reference for the street scene?
Meow...
The mysterious and elegant creature settles onto the prop box, looking up at the two girls with what seems to be displeasure.
Moira cannot help but bend down and reach out to it, but as she looks into its clear blue eyes she feels her mind go blank...
The next moment, the creature has vanished from the set.
Finally...
How could you idiots let it get away from right under your noses?!
Now, now, Marion.
I'm the one you're mad at. No need to take it out on the newcomers.
You...
If you're going to argue, take it outside. Or better yet, off the plate.
After all, no one expects professionalism out there.
Greta.
Back to work, people.
I thought you'd be more upset about the shoot being delayed.
I'm not THAT harsh, Steven. Besides, her appearance on set means we're in for a wonderful surprise when it's time to shoot.
'Her'?
Wait, you mean that creature?
Who else?
She's the companion of a guest of mine... and her showing up means that he's finally made up his mind.
He'll play the Messenger who appears in the next scene.
Isn't that right...
Lucian?
...As you wish.
Miss.Christine is free. None may interfere with her intentions or actions.
I apologize on her behalf.
Hold up, we're making a last minute cast change, just for some pretty boy no one's heard of?
It's only a minor role. No need to feel threatened, my handsome leading man Michael.
And it's not out of nowhere. Greta gave me a heads-up earlier.
Fine, fine.
I'm glad you've made your decision, Lucian. Film is a very different beast from stage, so I understand why you needed to take your time thinking it over.
But that costume fits you so well. I was right to invite you. I guarantee you'll be the next big thing in Wrankwood.
I can only hope.
Well, we've delayed enough. If the actors are ready, let's get on with the run-through.
I take it you've got your lines down, Lucian?
I could recite them backwards.
Alright then... The Death of Lady Dupont, front hall, scene four, action.
As the second Rockefeller pours his heart out to Lady Dupont on the balcony, a young Messenger from afar pushes open the front door.
Hello.
He's welcomed by Lady Dupont's brother, who always seems to have a lot on his mind—

Oh, for God's sake—where's Sir Dupont?!
—Dalton!
Where the Hitchcock has he run off to this time? Drinking again?
Where's my coordinator? Shelly?
We're here, we're here.
Thank goodness... *pant*... I found him... I brought Dalton!
(Drunken eyes)
(Hiccups)
I realized he was missing when I took a head count at the waiting area.
I searched every food cart, self-service bar, and outdoor rest area—anywhere that might have booze... I finally found him in some bushes by the road.
Knife... I found the *hic* knife!
He said he was looking for the knife prop that the Messenger is supposed to 'kill' Sir Dupont with.
So which one of you sent a drunk to go hunt down a prop? Where's the prop master, Moira?
I-I don't know...
Fine, whatever, as long as we've got the prop.
Costume crew! Get his clothes in order!
You do remember what you're playing in this scene, right?
Of course... a corpse. Nobody does it better. Haha.
Pathetic.
*hic* What'd ya say?
I'm sorry, but I simply can't stand people like you.
You used to be the darling of Wrankwood when pioneer films were in their heyday. Producers were queuing up to book you.
And now you're just going to let yourself rot, like mud in a pit?
Wh-What do you know?
I couldn't care less what you've been through. But at least show a shred of self-respect in front of the younger generation.
...That's one of the rules of Wrankwood, too.
You're playing opposite a man who's standing in front of a camera for the first time.
Fine, fine.
*hic*—Whasyourname?
Lucian. You may also call me... Phantom.
Whatever.
Sorry kid, didn't know this was your first shoot. But don't worry, just follow my lead. I can play this role hammered, no problem.
I'm gonna *hic* try to kill ya to shut ya up, 'cause of the news you brought...

We get to scuffling, then you grab this knife and stab me with it... It's a prop so the blade just slides into the handle. I got a blood bag in my chest pocket, and it'll pop with just a little pressure.
Just don't get stuck on thinking 'when am I gonna grab the knife', or you won't get in character. Got it?
You've read through that letter three times now, Sir Dupont.
If there's no issue with it, please assemble everyone in the household so I can read it to them.
Huh?
Dalton's eyes meet the young man's, and he swallows the drunken hiccup that had reached the front of his throat.
He sees not a rookie actor on a film set for the first time, but a Messenger who had come all the way from Victoria, his gaze tired but strong.
The studio falls silent. Director Quay has yet to call 'action', but Dalton can feel his counterpart's focus, and Abner's camera points towards them—
He is looking at a natural.
And so he takes a deep breath—
No, this is an official letter from Victoria's royal house. I can't let you read it aloud.
If people were to hear that the Dupont bloodline is all a lie, then my sister's marriage to any of the Rockefellers will be called off!
All that wealth, all that money that should have been mine, my very future... All gone in an instant, like popping a balloon.
Can't you just leave, boy? Pretend you were never here... I'll give you money. I'll give you all the money you want!
You have no authority over me.
You're just a Messenger! What part of this has anything to do with you?
The truth.
What?
True, what happens to this household has nothing to do with me. But I've got something to do with the truth, sir.
Even if I turned around and left right now, the truth of the Dupont family will still have kicked open your door, forevermore.
I have no power to take away your misfortune. All I can do is to announce its coming—whether you're willing to face it or not.
For such is my wretched fate!
Dalton looks into the young man's eyes and sees the deep pain and sorrow within.
Suddenly, he feels like he has never been so sober.
He does not need to recall his lines. They come to him naturally.
'Pain? Sorrow?'
'Laughable!'
'Who do you think you are?'
Dalton pulls his knife and swings it at the young man—
Sir Dupont and the young Messenger fight over the deadly weapon. It takes only a moment for it to change hands, their actions as coordinated as if they had rehearsed it a thousand times.
Pain?
Sorrow?
Laughable! Who do you think you are?
I will not let you ruin what should be mine!
As I said, sir, my only connection here is to the truth.
From the noble halls of Victoria, to the ruins of Gaul, to the Basilica of Laterano, to the coastline of Iberia...
I've walked all four corners of Terra, delivering the silent message of death, and bearing witness to countless meaningless tragedies. The story of the Duponts is but one of them.
And while I have never sought it, the truth always reveals itself before my eyes—
Such is my wretched fate!
Shut up!
Sir Dupont desperately lunges at the young man before him. The face of the Messenger has become twisted in the struggle.
He notices the young man pause for an instant, then grip the knife again. The point of the knife approaches his chest—
This man must have felt the weight of fate, he thinks.
A lifeless body falls to the ground. Blood, rose petals, the wind and rain.
Cut!
Did you get all that, Abner? I think we might be able to use this take as is!
Sure did!
(Claps)
Is this really this guy's first time on camera, Steven?
Greta said he used to be part of a theater troupe.
Oh, a stage actor...
Honestly, I'm more shocked by Dalton. I haven't seen such a convincing performance from him in ages.
Get up, Dalton! We're packing up. But if you want a drink tonight, I'm buying! Haha—
...Dalton?
Why isn't he getting up? Check on him, Shelly!
It is as if the air has frozen.
Dalton lies in front of the steps, not stirring even as Shelly dashes to his side.
The blood bag in his suit pocket has already run dry, but blood continues to seep from his chest.
Filled with a terrifying thought, Shelly reaches out toward the red liquid on the ground with a trembling hand—
It is warm, sticky, and metallic.
Th-This is real blood!
We've concluded that Dalton's death was an accident.
The medical team is taking his body back to central Wrankwood by short-haul transport. There they'll report the incident to the police.
Shelly has double-checked all the props just in case. So let's take a little break to pull ourselves together, and we'll resume shooting in the afternoon.
That soon?
Is there a problem?
Cameras and film can be replaced, but we're talking about a dead man here. We've only just wiped up the blood...
How the hell did he go looking for a prop and end up with a real knife?
Did someone trick that washed-up drunk of an actor into looking for a lost prop himself? Or did someone put it in his hands on purpose?
H-Hey now, let's not get carried away here.
Sure, the old man was a drunk and late to the set all the time, but it's not like he had any enemies in the crew. Why would anyone go that far?
Besides, it's just an ordinary knife. There are plenty of them lying around in the crew kitchen, in the convenience stores, even our own rooms...
Maybe someone just mentioned it in passing to Dalton, and then he...
All I'm saying is that it's suspicious, okay?
Then why don't you ask Dalton yourself, Miss Detective?
Look, I know you're all shocked at losing a coworker. I feel the same way. But there's something even more important we should be worrying about—
We don't have much time left for the shoot.
I'm sure you haven't forgotten that this studio plate is kept separate from the nomadic city.
While it's still controlled by the central command tower, the film crew gets direct authority over certain functions, allowing us to drive it outside of central Wrankwood.
And once the lease ends, the studio closes down, and the plate returns on its own.
But more space means more costs. According to the contract, extending the lease will result in an expensive penalty—
Which will be reflected in all of your paychecks.
As the producer, it's my job to act in everyone's best interests and ensure the film crew can function.
I agree.
So do I, as both the lead actress and investor.
Miss Detective here did remind me, though, that someone must indeed answer for this.
Prop Master?
Y-Yes, ma'am?
It was your responsibility to manage the props, and you've clearly neglected it. Even your intern was on set before you.
I-I'm sorry, ma'am.
Apologies won't cut it, darling.
You're fired. I suggest looking for a new job before our plate gets too far from the city... And whatever you're about to say, save it. You're getting off easy and you know it.
You're in charge of the prop team now, Moira.
M-Me?
Or you could leave with your former boss, but I trust you're smarter than that.
As for you, Steven, it's time to get your act together.
We both agree that The Death of Lady Dupont lacks a strong ending.
You were supposed to submit the final script for those scenes last week. The shooting is almost caught up with what's written.
I have my hands full directing, okay? This crew doesn't exactly inspire confidence, you know...
So you still haven't found the inspiration?
...Yeah.
It's your own original script, darling. Your baby. It deserves the very best ending.
That's my biggest concern as well.
In the end, which of the Rockefeller boys kills Lady Dupont? Where? How? What are her last words?
The ending will determine the quality of the whole film.
She needs a good, proper death to kickstart the revival of my career in Wrankwood.
That's the only reason I've poured my entire fortune into this project.
...God will come find me.
What?
Oh, in literary criticism we often say that inspiration is like a god descending upon the author, using him as a vessel to write about death and rebirth, destruction and hope...
Anyway, don't worry, I'll finish the story.
Good. Then you're dismissed, so get to work.
Miss Stone! Miss Stone!
I need to talk to you about something! It's important—
I know what you want to say, Shelly, but now's not the time.
I have to go check on Lucian. This is his first film, and his first day on set, and something this serious happens...
I can't have him running away on us, now can I?
As for whatever you're thinking... you're overthinking it.
I... I'm in charge of the whole prop team?
Omigosh, congrats, Molly!
I mean, it's a shame about Dalton—may he rest in peace—but still, I'm happy for you.
I've been working here in Wrankwood for way longer than you, but I'm still stuck doing backgrounds, while you just got the chance of a lifetime! Life really is like a box of chocolates, huh!
That's because you always put so much of yourself into your work. The art director isn't comfortable letting you handle more.
But I'm just a girl from the pioneer zone who's good with her hands and came here looking for a job. Can I really do this...?
If the producer says you can, you can! She's never wrong about people.
Your sister will be happy, won't she? You came to Wrankwood because of her, right? You gotta tell her you've been promoted to prop master!
I'll call her after we clock out. Miss Stone put her trust in me, so I have to do this right.
That's my girl! You're already sounding the part. I bet in just a few days, you won't even have time to watch horror flicks with me anymore.
Tippi!
Stop walking in front of me, Shelly!
You already checked the storyboard three times and the equipment five times. I swore to the producer that I wouldn't lose another set of cameras. Now can you stop glaring at me like that?
(Whispers) Do you think... it was really...?
What was that? Speak up.
Do you think Dalton's death was really just an accident? Doesn't it seem strange to you?
Don't know, don't care.
I've lost count of how many times I've had to work overtime because that has-been got lost in a beer keg. Good riddance.
He wasn't like that five years ago.
No shit, he was at the top of the world, starring in Greta and Steven's next big thing. How could I forget that when we were both there?
That's the weird part!
I saw a lot of familiar names on the staff list during the planning meetings. And when I saw your face, I got this eerie feeling, like—
Like we were back five years ago, witnessing that young actress's death again?
Doesn't it all seem a little... similar?
How so? One was a fall, the other was a prop mix-up.
You're overreacting, Shelly.
I'm telling you, I'm not crazy... Ah, now I remember! Dalton was the one most responsible for the accident!
Judgment has come... Someone came for him...
Just stop already! Stop scratching your arm, and stop coming up with this nonsense! I told you: if what happened still bothers you that much, get therapy and stay away from the set.
We're just ordinary joes shooting crappy movies for a living. Nobody gives a flip about us. Shelly? Do you hear me, Shelly?
I hear it... I hear it... Something's coming... coming for us...
Picture the position and angle of the stab in your mind.

Near the bottom-right of the abdomen is a gap between vital organs, rich in blood supply, but not immediately lethal. You can feed off the blood of others, and let it herald the climax that is to come.
Or, you can move the point of the knife up and penetrate the thorax.
The lungs are fragile and offer no resistance. Your target will drown in both his own blood and your animated monologue.
Raise your dagger, boy.
Think back to the segments we've rehearsed. You've done well. There's only one step to go.
Stab! Let the point of the knife know how it feels to stir the organs and sever blood vessels, and let it listen to the sound of the heart beating!
Are you afraid?
...How ordinary.
He's not afraid. He just doesn't understand.
He's still not willing to practice stabbing. He hasn't spoken a single word since he took up the dagger.
Go prepare for tomorrow's performance.
...I will instruct him myself.
I see... Understood.
Do you find the dagger heavy?
...Yes.
But its weight does not come from the metal, nor from the ornaments. It comes from your heart. Do you understand?
The boy's hair covers his eyes. He nods in silence.
Whiteflower does not realize that you've learned so quickly. You're waiting for her to provide answers she cannot give you.
Listen to me, child...
The Troupe Leader holds Lucian's little hands in his own. His smile is warm and gentle, but his iron grip permits no refusal.
Lucian can feel his stiff fingers begin to relax.
Forget the techniques that Whiteflower's been trying to teach you. You have no need to imitate any form, or any method of stabbing.
There is no dagger without blood on it, no crossbow that is not fired. Just hold them.
They are not props. They will tell you what to desire.
Let them guide you. Do not doubt them, even if they point you towards someone you never expected—a friend, a lover, even yourself...
Perhaps even me, standing before you now.
You... you?
Have no hesitation. Stab.
Give it a try.
(Shakes head)
Try it.
(Shakes head)
Try it.
Then you, and I...
What roles are we playing?
If we're forgetting the techniques, then forget the characters and the script as well. They're simply the medium of theater, not theater itself.
Lucian grips the knife. The Troupe Leader lets go of his hand and kneels in front of him, so that their gazes are almost level.
But we're too close to each other...
Are you afraid you'll hurt me?
It's alright, child.
Forget even that feeling.
You will immerse yourself in theater, and touch upon its beauty.
Gazing at the Troupe Leader, Lucian finds himself overcome with doubt. Is this really the first time he has seen the man?
He does not understand what he is saying. He begins to doubt whether the Feline before him even exists at all.
Lucian can feel his own heart throbbing.
The heartbeat never lies. The heartbeat knows the answer.
How should he ask it?
The Troupe Leader smiles, encouraging him wordlessly.
The answer is obvious.
Without hesitation, Lucian thrusts—
Did you feel it?
Lucian's hands are trembling. He feels them grow weak, almost letting go of the dagger.
The Troupe Leader has not moved an inch, not even a flicker of expression.
Lucian is certain his knife found its mark in the man's body. He heard a sound... felt the pain that should not have belonged to him, a strong, willful pain.
He had been silent since he came to the Troupe. Now, though, the pain makes him want to sing.
This is your first lesson. All stories leave behind only certain emotions, or...
The Troupe Leader pauses. Lucian casts a questioning glance, and meets those golden eyes.
A bond so tight that it cannot be broken.
You're a natural actor. You should study with me.
Think about it, then come and find me... I will never be an obstacle to you, Lucian.
Lucian looks down at his hands.
The blood on his gloves has been washed away, but he remembers the feeling of taking Dalton's knife out of his hands.
Fascination, revelry.
The complex emotions he felt when he was taught to use a dagger for the first time.
Forget complicated assassination techniques. Ignite the ego with intense emotion, until all that is left is two souls tearing at one another.
Let life converge upon the knife of truth and falsehood, and bestow upon the target the perfect ending—
Death.
Come in.
Lucian.
Madam Stone.
You look pale... are you alright?
For our friends joining us for the first time, if you're here for exquisite Yanese calligraphy or rare Sargonian treasures, I suggest you save yourself some time and leave now.
This here is a Script Auction, unique to Wrankwood, where unfinished ideas, drafts, and their associated copyrights are up for sale.
As such, unlike traditional auctions where anything put up must have its authenticity verified, we offer no guarantees as to the value of the items—
Whether it has marketing potential or artistic value, whether it will become Wrankwood's next timeless classic or a forgotten flop...
That is for you, and you alone, to decide.

To protect the intellectual property of the items on auction, you are asked to put away your terminals and turn off any recording equipment. And without further ado, allow me to introduce the first item—
The Death of Lady Dupont, a story of love and hatred taking place in Columbia, involving two blue-blood families in changing times. Bidding starts at one hundred thousand gold certificates.
One hundred thousand. We have a bid for one hundred thousand from the gentleman in the back... One hundred and fifty thousand from the pretty young lady on my left! Anyone else?
One hundred and fifty thousand going once...
One hundred and fifty thousand going twice...
Six hundred thousand.
(Whispers) Miss Stone! Are you here to ruin my auction? Placing such a high bid for the first item will only make the rest of it seem like an afterthought!
I have no interest in slowly inching up the price. I simply bid what it's worth to me.
I understand that it's your style to gamble lavishly on scripts, but think of your finances... Didn't quite a few of your recent films flop? Are you sure it's wise to spend so much?
I can afford your fee. Smack that hammer of yours, old friend.
You have a rather talented young man here, and I'm eager to have a conversation with him.
Whiskey, please, no ice.
Would you like one too, my friend?
My doctor has advised me to refrain from drink.
That accent... are you from Sargon?
Interesting. That stern, sullen complexion of yours could not be more Victorian.
(Sargonian) I have tread the sands of Sargon.
(King's Victorian) But I was indeed born in Victoria.
You switch between languages at ease, and your accents are flawless. But from what I can see in your habits, those aren't the only two countries you're familiar with.
I've been to Bolívar, Siracusa, Leithanien, Rim Billiton... and now, Wrankwood.
—The entertainment capital of Terra. Pleasant weather and lots of sunshine—the perfect place to shoot movies. So what brings you here?
Research? Pleasure? Or perhaps you're like everyone else who came to seek fame and glory, most of whom end up at the extras market, fighting over bit roles to make ends meet?
I'm looking for someone.
I've tracked his shadows across many lands, over the span of many years.
Oh? Who? I know all the film crews, whether they work downtown or on the nomadic plates. Maybe I could be of help.
That won't be necessary.
I see, fair enough... What a shame though...
If there's nothing else—
My name is Greta Stone. I'm a producer here at Wrankwood... a rather well-known one in the industry, if I might add.
I took you for the son of some noble, or a film school student who came to try your luck, and was hoping to invite you to join my film crew.
Everyone who comes to the Wrankwood Script Auction tends to have a passion for artistry, and I must admit you have a rather unique aura about you.
I was once an actor for a small theater troupe in Victoria.
I knew I was right. You were born for the silver screen!
But I suppose that's all moot if you have other business to attend to. I guess I'll be going home empty-handed.
Can I see the script?
Of course!
This rough draft is just the starting line. I'll give it the time, manpower, and money it needs to become a masterpiece that will leave its mark on cinema history.
And I guarantee that you'll shine, even with just a minor role in a single scene.

No one told me about any of these new scenes. Aren't I supposed to be the director of photography?
And why do we need to set up cameras at the edge of the plate? There aren't any sets out there... wait, I get it.
What the Ford, Greta?! You planned all this! Why did you hide it from us?
Calm down, Abner... Maybe she just didn't have time to update you on the new schedule, in the chaos following Shelly's death...
You're still defending this lunatic? She wants to get the plate close to a Catastrophe to film it! Putting all of our lives at risk!
The furious cameraman smashes his equipment to the ground.
I've had enough of this shit! I'm leaving this place NOW!
As impulsive as ever, Abner.
Real persuasive coming from a goddamn lunatic!

How much of your own money did you put into this, to make up for the box office losses of that movie five years ago? You're counting on this one for your comeback after all those failed investments...
So if this film flops, are you gonna use Dalton and Shelly's accidents too, the same way you used that girl who fell to her death?
Calm down, Abner.
I see you've been closely following my career, but what about yours? How many films have you worked on in the last three years? This is the first, isn't it?
Sloppy, short-tempered, unprofessional... Camera equipment is expensive—what other film crew would even give you a chance? You wouldn't even make it as a freelance event photographer.
You want to leave? Fine by me. I'll even waive the contract penalty, for old times' sake. But where do you think you can go, Abner?
Does it matter? Worse comes to worst—
What, you leave town? Change jobs? Spare all those poor camera lenses from your abuse? Say whatever you like, swear as many oaths as you want—
If that's really how you feel, why are you even here?
So are you leaving? Or not?
Abner does not pick up the equipment, nor does he leave. He stands unmoving in silence, caught between his life and his past.
Have you calmed down?
Look on the bright side. If we do finish this movie, maybe you'll win a few more awards for Best Cinematography. Feel free to reject me once the other offers start rolling in.
Dream as big as you want, Greta.
I stopped dreaming five years ago.
Now, these latest two accidents have reminded some of us of the tragedy we've experienced together, and led others to be taken in by half-true rumors.
So I'd like to take this chance to lay everything out on the table, for the sake of our cooperation on the rest of this project.
Most of the crew here also worked with me five years ago, when an unfortunate accident occurred due to aging equipment—
A young actress, Laura, was killed.
Feel free to look up the police reports. Everything is out in the open. I have nothing to hide.
But the only connection that incident has to my decision to film a Catastrophe is that it taught me a lesson.
What we lack is not skill. It's courage.
Of course, by no means have I disregarded your safety.
Before our plate departed central Wrankwood, I visited expert Catastrophologists at Trimounts University.
I obtained a detailed report, and tested the data in the plate's navigational system multiple times. The studio plate will keep a safe distance from the Catastrophe.
Access to the emergency escape landboats will remain unrestricted. Any staff member who feels their life is in danger may use them at your own discretion.
And I will be the last to leave this plate.
If there are no further questions. Dismissed.
Not you. Stay.
M-Miss Stone...
I-I won't apologize for telling everyone!
No need to be anxious. I just wanted to know how you got the documents.
Like I said, I did it myself—
Don't lie to me. You have no talent for it, and I have no time.
...I'm obliged to protect the identity of my informants.
Disturbing the peace of the studio is a violation of the second and fifth clauses of your contract. Would you like to see the outcome of that reflected in your bank account?
But there are exceptions made under exceptional circumstances—
(Whispers) It was Lucian.
Greta casts her gaze across the crowd and scattered equipment, and sees him standing outside the chaos.
The Feline receives Greta's cold gaze unperturbed, and gives her a nod.
You said Michael came to see you last night?
...Yeah.
But we kept going after Shelly left, didn't we? We watched a Yanese jiangshi movie next. ...Wait, come to think of it, you did leave the room for a little while.
Michael sent me a message on my terminal. He said he was waiting for me in the studio, because he had some important questions.
I'm sorry. I shouldn't have left in the middle of our movie night. Please don't be mad...
I'm mad alright...
Tippi...
I'm mad you didn't ask me to come with you.
Huh?

What if Michael tried to assault you? That sleazebag wouldn't know morals or self-restraint if they hit him in the face. Or, what if he was frustrated about the shoot getting interrupted and wanted to take it out on you? You're, like, a magnet for bullies.
Hey, I don't bully girls.
Yeah right. You're an asshole even to Marion, never mind other girls.
Heh, even the kid sees right through you.
I wanted to ask you to come, but he said I had to come alone...
I've been in a few film crews. I could guess it was some kind of prank. But I didn't want you to get caught up in it too.
Molly...
I've been in Wrankwood way longer than you have, and I know way more jobs. Next time you meet someone like that, just tell me, and I'll help you think of a way to get back at them.
Alright.
Well, what now, Detective? With Moira's testimony, can I take it I've been cleared of suspicion?
Maybe it really was a coincidence that he ran into Shelly.
We saw how she was when she left last night...
Like she wasn't all there, right?
Yeah. Maybe she was at her limit...
But...
Thank you for testifying on my behalf, Moira.
I know it took courage. After all, Tip might be even more protective of you than your actual sister...
Speaking of which, is she still working as an actress? If she's as cute as you... do you think you could give me her number? Maybe I could help her land a role or two.
Seriously? Even after getting accused of murder?
Come on, babe, I'm just trying to lighten the mood.
Whatever. Don't change the subject.
You still haven't told me why you were looking for Moira.
Yeah, I wanna know too.
I think they deserve to know, Michael.
Moira, don't—
He asked me if there was a way to sneak off the plate.
Mr. Dalton! You really got that extra scene for me! And it's a wire stunt to boot?
How did you get Director Quay to agree so easily, when he wouldn't budge when I asked him?
Greta personally recruited me as lead actor. Steven wouldn't dare turn down my requests.
This is my first time in a wire rig... I'll never forget this opportunity you gave me! Okay, I have to go prepare now!
Sure, go on.
Now you're acting all humble? Might've even fooled me, if I didn't already know you were trying to get Steven to mess with my role behind my back.
First time in a wire rig, huh... Good to hear.
Rookies should know their places. Now you'll be a good sport and take it, right? Even if you're left hanging for hours?
Shelly! I just got another scene and I need the wire rig. Can you prepare it for me?
I know you're passionate about acting. But how many extra scenes is this now? Do you even realize how much you've screwed with the schedule I made for the entire crew?
Do you feel even an ounce of guilt, Princess? I'm not changing the entire schedule again because of you. This crew doesn't revolve around you.
I'm sorry, Shelly. I'm not trying to increase your workload, honest. But Mr. Dalton said the director signed off on it...
Dalton did?
Alright then.
Thanks, Shelly!
(He was just telling me how you have no respect for your seniors in the field. No way did he do this out of pure kindness.)
(The wire rig, huh?)
Hello, Laura! You look wonderful today! Not many actors would be so happy to do an aerial stunt!
Well, I've never been so excited! This scene will be the only one in the movie that belongs entirely to me. I want to knock it out of the ballpark!
Well, aren't you just adorable?
Try not to get hurt though. And if you're tired, you can always come find me in my room. I'll be there all night...
Oh, Marion! Have you come to touch up Michael's makeup?
I have to go prepare. See you later!
Yeah, see you in hell, bitch!
D-Don't be angry with me, Mari.
I look away for one moment and you're flirting with another woman! Are you tired of me already?
You've been making eyes at her on the set too... If you like younger women that much, how about you let her do your makeup from now on?
Please, babe, I'm serious about you.
I won't talk to her from now on, I swear!
...You swear?
I swear!
I'm familiar with your vices by now, but this is an important time for your career. Don't screw it up, and don't let me down.
That Laura girl is a loud-mouthed show-off. I'm sure she's up to something. You can do better.
Keep your distance from her.
No talking to her, and no physical contact. Even if she's dying right in front of you, that's none of your business... got it?
Babe?
He can tell from Marion's eyes that she is not joking.
Alright, I promise.
It's still not my turn? What's going on?
There's something on Mr. Dalton's end, and everyone on the set's gone over. No one's giving orders now... Look, we've even got that rookie still hanging up there.
Oh, poor thing.
You never struck me as the compassionate type. Trying to get her in bed?
Hey, you know me.
Let me go check on our poor little beauty...
I promised Mari this morning that I wouldn't approach her, but...
We never said anything about her coming to me, right?
Michael looks up and sees Laura's slender frame hanging in mid-air. Her dripping sweat has formed a small puddle on the floor.
She is tired, but her eyes remain strong, stinging Michael's frivolous heart.
The wire rig is creaking. From experience, Michael can tell there is something wrong with it.
However—
All the better for me, yeah?
She'll call for help once she realizes something's not right.
I'll be her hero and have the perfect excuse for a little tête-à-tête, all without breaking my promise to Mari.
I'll let her hang a little longer.
Why aren't you calling for help, Laura? I'm right here, you know!
It's been hours, and the rope's sounding increasingly off... Where did this equipment even come from? How could it be so ancient?
Is that a crack around the belt? She has to have noticed that!
No, I have to tell her, I have to...
He walks forward, stepping in the puddle of sweat. The heat of the limelight is causing Laura to become dehydrated.
Michael will never understand her determination, her passion. It is as though she is denying everything he is.
Ahem.
Michael turns around to find Marion standing in the far corner, gazing at him and the exhausted actress with cold eyes.
'Even if she's dying right in front of you, that's none of your business... got it?'
Michael strokes his orange ears and stops in his tracks.
...Well, it really is none of my business if she chooses to suffer.
That's your first lesson, Laura.
Never be too serious. Always put yourself first, even if you have to hurt others. That's the most important thing—
Creak, creak.
The rope above his head is being stretched to its limit.
You're still not going to call for help?
Aaaahhhhhh—!!
Michael?
What's going on? What were you thinking about?
I came with you to the makeup room for an explanation, not to watch you melt down.
What did Moira mean when she said you wanted to leave the plate?
We have to get out of here, babe!

lines.st: Victoria's famous Crimson Troupe is here in Daremont for three days! Don't miss it!

lines.x: It's the first time you've left the castle and traveled so far with the troupe, isn't it?
Mhm.
Are you nervous?
...I'm fine, Master.
The others are playing the leads. I'm just a very minor role, watching from the side in most of my scenes...
When the true performance unfolds before your eyes, it is not easy to resist the urge to run.
You've learned another lesson today, child.

lines.st: Police statement on the Daremont Case:
At this time, there is no evidence linking the case to the Crimson Troupe which passed through the area recently. The police has established a special task force to investigate.

lines.x: A spire in Lupukarn, Leithanien
(Claps)
A splendid performance.
You've finally made a breakthrough after your first performance as the lead.
The tiny black crystal on your neck is almost perfectly hidden. The audience sees only the glamorous star, not the Infected approaching death one step at a time.
Your performance is increasingly mature, and your songs move the passions. There is virtually no stiffness, no pretentiousness.
Are you complimenting me?
Of course.
Are you not going? Or do you want to stay, and take in the aftertaste of the stage?
Aftertaste?
You mean the taste of blood?
Excuse me, Mister, please let me through.
I want to go home.
I think the Crimson Troupe can take her in.
Take her in?
She doesn't know that her home is gone. She doesn't know that some of the people before her still have her family's blood on them...
That is what we'd call a fateful encounter, is it not?
As it was with you and the Troupe.
Perhaps you should teach her yourself. Teach her how to blend in, how to embrace a new life... how to walk the path that you have.
The show goes on, even once we've left the stage?
Ah, you remember what I said.
Yes, there is no drama like life. The aesthetics of destiny are never bound by any medium.
...Mister?
What do you think, Lucian?
Have you decided? You're in a position to make a choice now, aren't you?
Are you awake?
(Lazy yawn)
What's the matter?
The bitterness of obsession... your emotions.
A challenge to the palate?
I wouldn't say that.
But your flashbacks have become increasingly frequent since we came to this plate.
That means we're getting closer to him.
You noticed the strangeness of that coordinator.
As did you.
Unfortunately, she was visited by tragedy before I could extract any more clues.
You used that camera assistant to break the news of the Catastrophe?
It was too much a coincidence for that document to show up when it did, the way it did.
When one's been in enough plays, it's not hard to realize that such an obvious clue is bound to be a trigger.
Dalton's death was only the beginning.
The plot devices have been set, and their developments foreshadowed. The curtain has been raised, and it cannot be lowered again.
And the author will not appear until the story reaches the climax.
...Only then can I kill him.
(Yawn)
Be careful, Lucian.
I know.
What I mean is, be careful of yourself.
As I said, your emotions are filled with the bitterness of obsession... It's a dangerous thing.
I apologize, Miss.Christine.
You will not have to endure it for long.
Last night wasn't the first time Michael's come looking for you, was it?
You haven't been in Wrankwood for long, and you're certainly not the type who would have meddled with something so far outside the scope your duties, if he hadn't been pestering you.
He came to me the day after Dalton died and I took over the prop team.
He wanted to leave without going through the normal process, and without anyone noticing.
Without, like, telling Miss Stone or going through the contract cancellation and exit procedures?
But he's the lead actor. Someone was bound to find out, and he'd have to pay the contract penalty anyway.
Unless... something here scared him more than paying the penalty.
He said since the prop team had access to all the tools and was familiar with the structure of the plate, he wondered if I could...
If you could think of a way.
Yeah.
I mean, I'm sure you could, but Michael's not done with his scenes yet. If he really got away and Miss Stone found out you helped him... she wouldn't let you off the hook easy.
She could even end your career in Wrankwood!
What would your sister think?
My sister...
Didn't you say the rest of your family was against you coming to Wrankwood, and she's the only one who cheered you on?
You have to make it, and not just to prove your family wrong.
I just don't want to disappoint my sister. I want her to be happy for me.
S-So don't be mad, Tippi...
Please?
I'm just mad that you were hiding stuff from me.
I know you'd never agree to Michael's demands. You just didn't know how to refuse him... I can help with that.
Alright, Tippi. I've never seen you so angry...
Sh-Should we watch a movie together?
*Sigh*...
Sure.
...You want to sneak off the plate with me?
Yeah, babe. After we spend some quality time here, we can go and pack up right away.
Even if that prop girl really does find a way for you to circumvent the exit procedures... how do you expect to avoid the penalty for breaching your contract?
Shooting isn't over yet, and you know Greta. She'll make you pay an arm and a leg!
Who cares about some stupid contract when there's a killer on the loose?!
Dalton's dead, Shelly's dead... how long do you think it'll be before you and I are next?
We both watched and did nothing when Laura died five years ago.
Whether the killer is her lover, her dad, or some self-righteous vigilante, he's clearly after everyone who was involved!
He wants us to repent and pay for what we did!
I can take you away from this awful place, babe. We have our whole future ahead of us.
Ha, how romantic of you.
But since when has your love been so deep?
What?
So even when you were winking at Melanie, and flirting with Anna from stage design and Mila from logistics...
You're telling me you were seriously thinking about spending the rest of your life with me?
D-Does that matter right now?
Of course it does—I'm not going, Michael.
And neither are you.
You know how hard it was to get you a lead role in one of Greta's films? And not just one, but three at once!
Play this right, and you'll be a bona fide Wrankwood movie star!
There'll be another chance...
Look at me, Michael.
Your costume in The Death of Lady Dupont is even more exquisite and eye-catching than Melanie's, the title character.
Your perfume, what you wore to fashion week—I bought all of that with my own money. Even those fake ears and tail that let you pass as a Vulpo came from me...
Without me, the public will find out that this 'Michael' product suddenly doesn't seem so shiny anymore.
We've been together for five years. I gave up everything for you. I've spent so much of my savings, and I'm not getting any younger... I can't afford to wait for 'another chance'.
I love you, Michael. So don't let me down.
You... love me?
You were glad when Melanie publicly revealed our relationship the day Dalton died, weren't you?
To everyone else, I'm the troublemaking playboy, while you're the poor, suffering girlfriend who gave everything only to get nothing in return but mockery.
How very sympathetic, huh?
And what part of that is a lie?
You said it yourself: you're just investing in a product. Investment costs money.
You'd have invested in Dalton instead, wouldn't you, if he hadn't become a hopeless drunk?
You're just a parasite looking for a host, Marion.
This is stupid... And I'm stupid too.
Well, I guess that's it for us. You can stay if you want—I'm outta here.
Oh, and here's a tip: if you're looking for your next investment, try that pretty boy Lucian. He caught the entire crew's attention his first time in front of a camera. He's the real deal.
The moment you walk out of here, I'm reporting you to Greta.
You cold-hearted lunatic. Are you not afraid of the killer?
And why should I be?
Five years ago, I didn't even know why Laura fell to her death until later.
You're the one who watched and did nothing, not me.
You...
What are you looking at, Michael?
Rage overcomes the 'Vulpo' like a tidal wave. He sees a fire axe in a corner of the makeup room and clenches his fists.
Ah—
Why would they go down into the power deck?! Sure, there were mannequins all over the surface, but there had to have been a better plan, right Molly?
I agree.
There's nowhere to run from the Wraith in the tight spaces of a nomadic city's underground structure.
In the end, it found them one by one and hung them from the ducts to await a slow death in the endless darkness...
I went to one of the cast's interviews, and they said it was inspired by the rituals of certain Columbian wasteland tribes. Something about atonement and judgment...
Well, how they got there was kinda dumb, but I did like the segment in the power deck.
Why?
Because it was all shot on location!
Rumor has it that they ran out of cash, so they snuck into another film's studio plate and finished the shooting there... Exciting, right?
Wait... but I've seen this movie in theaters. Why don't I remember any of these scenes?
Because we're watching a copy of the original reel that was sent to the ratings agencies!
How did you even get your hands on something like that?
It's, like, a basic skill for a true horror fan!
You're hiding something, Tippi...
You know so much about the industry and all the ins-and-outs of Wrankwood. How does a background painter get so much insider knowledge?
How can you do this to me, Molly?!
I share my super-secret treasure with you, hoping to make you happy, and all I get is an interrogation? *Sob*...
Aw, don't cry, I won't ask anymore, alright?
Let's just focus on the movie... so stop looking away from the screen!
Heh, you noticed.
Sorry, there's just been so much going on with the film crew lately, I'm... I'm a little nervous...
Don't worry, I'm here for you, girl!
You sure are!
Feeling better now?
Yeah!
Okay, listen up, Molly.
No matter who I am, one thing's for sure: I'm your best friend on this crew.
So if there's a killer on the loose, I'll protect you!
And if work's not going well, we can do it together. We'll make our names as Wrankwood's golden horror duo, Tippi the spray-painter and Moira the prop-maker!
And when we're really close, I'll take you to meet my family... Although I don't know if you'll be scared or surprised!
Well...
Then I'll introduce you to my sister too!
We'll share stories of Wrankwood while she peels us apples. She can peel one without breaking the skin in under half a minute! I know you'll love her!
Now there's an offer I can't refuse!
The old TV lights up both the darkened room and their faces. Terrifying sounds echo throughout, but take nothing away from their smiles.
They cuddle up against one another, fighting their fear with a much more familiar 'fear', and dreaming of the future of their budding friendship.
It is a rare, quiet evening.
Why is the set so dark?
*Sigh* I should've asked Moira. She's always here early, and she knows where all the switches are.
The prop room isn't that far, is it? Have I been going around in circles? ...Something doesn't seem right.
Huh? Am I hearing things?
The sound of water dripping echoes through the empty set as though answering Hautin's question, but such 'response' serves only to increase her confusion and fear.
She knows there are no water pipes above her, but the sound she is hearing is definitely coming from nearby.
Wh-Who's there?!
Is this another one of those stupid pranks you'll laugh at me over drinks after work for?
C-Come out now and I won't hold it against you! Or don't tell me Moira's in on it too? She's just a kid, how could you make her—
Ouch—

Hautin falls backwards to the ground. Only then does she realize that she is wearing a flashlight on her belt. But she barely has time to laugh at herself before she smells a strong, sweet scent accompanied by a tang of iron.
Frightened, she stands up and turns on her flashlight—
A breeze sways the ropes. Hanging down from them are two familiar and strangely quiet figures.
The two lovers, Michael and Marion, subject of much of the crew's gossip, have met their deaths with their backs against each other.
Through the faint light, Hautin sees their faces, their expressions, the way they sway through the air, and the dancing specks of dust...
It is indescribably eerie, and somehow... sacred.
She remembers what Tippi said when she joined one of her movie sessions...
In the rituals of certain Columbian wasteland tribes, hanging represents both judgment of the dead, and a warning for the living.
Until the scorching sun cracks their corpses open, until all believe they deserved their punishment, until the ropes can no longer bear the weight of their sin—
Thump.
H-Help! Somebody! Anybody!
The unlucky stagehand runs outside. As she does, a thought flashes through her mind—
What an absurd morning.
Psst, hey. You saw Michael and Marion's bodies too, right?
Dude! Don't remind me, I've been trying to forget! It was freaking terrifying!
M-My hands are still shaking so much, I don't think I can hold the boom mic...
You're still worried about work?! When there's an actual serial killer on set right now?
So... let's skedaddle?
You want to run? Did you already forget how Michael and Marion ended up like that?
Michael even had his suitcase right under him. I heard he'd been packed and ready to go for quite a while. Even found himself a secret passage to leave through...
And he still was murdered right before he left the plate.
No way that killer's gonna let any of us leave.
It's just like the plot of Jack The Liberipper. This set is now his hunting ground, and any one of us could very well be his prey.
Abner, how long has it been since you inspected your chest rig?
Look, there's a bunch of loose screws on your mechanical arm. One little slip, and that camera's gonna be shards of glass all over the floor...
If it breaks, it breaks.
At least let me take a look at your camera lens then.
I'm good.
I'm your assistant, you know.
Don't think I don't see what you're actually up to—you best stay away from me, kiddo.
Dalton's death was no accident, right? The same goes for Shelly, Michael, and Marion...
There's no way the victims were chosen at random. All of them were involved in that incident five years ago.
You should be working for one of those gossip rags, not behind a camera.

Who said they were mutually exclusive? Reporters make more dough though... Anyway, Abner, my good man, you were there five years ago, right? C'mon, you can tell me. Not like we'll be doing anything else today.
Miss Rutherford, you have no lines in this scene...
Then do you suppose I could simply stay in my room researching which exact foundation shade Lady Dupont should be wearing before she dies?
That will be decided after we finish the climatic scenes at the end, which are still... I'm sorry...
Ah, so that god of yours is running tardy. But I'm in no mood to blame your god right now, my dear.
Were we not so far from the city, a major incident like this would have the Wrankwood police locking down the plate and crawling all over our set. And naturally, the shooting would be suspended.
Not to say that our current situation is any better.
Steven, I'm starting to worry that my investment in this project will come to nothing.
How much do you or Greta know about what's transpired these past few days, that I don't?
Oh, this is where you two were.
So where's the producer? Greta owes us an explanation!
Whispers abound.
Gossip permeates the set, with not a single soul at work.
Three homicides, and four victims. The spate of murders has everyone overcome with fear, anger, confusion, and shock... The whole film crew is on the verge of losing control.
The clamor sends even her own heart into a mess. She hates the feeling.
Greta struts right into the center of the studio, and switches on the brightest bulb. The piercing light stuns everyone into silence, and everyone's attention fixes on her.
She takes a deep breath.
I have some announcements to make, everyone.
I've never received any training as an actor, so you might have to come closer to hear me.
You'll want to check your personal Wrankwood financial terminals in a few minutes. I've instructed finance to send out everyone's remaining wages.
Of course, the families of Dalton, Shelly, Michael, and Marion will also receive compensation for their losses, which I will handle personally later.

Yes, The Death of Lady Dupont has yet to conclude filming, and your contracts are still valid, so none of this should even be necessary. But I can count on one hand how many of you are still in the mood to work.
Hah, this is so unlike you, Greta.
We've simply run into a few... considerable hiccups. But this is no time to thrown in the towel.
Or has this experience made our producer lose confidence in the quality of the film?
Throwing in the towel? You're overthinking it, darling.
I've never once doubted my own judgment, much less considered abandoning the project.
Then why are you—
I'd like you to gather round for a story, everyone.
Hey, kid...
Me, Miss Stone?
Take out your pen recorder, notebook, shorthand terminal, whatever... Then sit down, and listen.
Wh-What do you mean?
I've seen you sneaking around with those toys, and I don't appreciate it. But feel free to record everything I'm about to say.
Take it to Trimounts Entertainment Weekly, or Wrankwood Coffee Time, or Persons Magazine... I don't care, as long as you get the story out. I won't hold you accountable either.
See it as a reward, or a punishment. It doesn't matter to me.
I take it everyone here is familiar with the Wrankwood Academy Awards.

The Best Newcomer award. Every year, countless students of film and television schools, as well as amateur film buffs, submit their entries in a bid to prove how much they understand their creations and the market.
They crave for their work to be chosen, because even the lowest award still implies their film is up to Wrankwood's standards... and a certain Phidia was no exception.

She believed herself to be an auteur with both the brains and the will to realize her dreams. She was certain she would one day stand on Wrankwood's red carpet, even if she had to temporarily settle for life in a cramped rental.
So she waited for the very best story and the very best partners, then she put together a crew and brought that story to life. Submitted it.
But this no-name newcomer craved success too much, not to mention the pressure of living up to the expectations of her companions.
As the voting period was ending, her obsession intensified, along with her doubts about the quality of the work she had submitted.
She could not rationalize her strange thoughts. She begged both friends and family, and raised a large sum—large for her then, at least—before knocking on the judges' doors.
Why does this sound so familiar...?
On the very day of the Academy Awards, the committee publicized what the Phidia had done, and in so doing proved Wrankwood's professionalism and purity to the whole of Terra.
And so she was disqualified. Her crew cut all ties with her, and she lost everything she had.
Now I remember!
It was so ironic, because the academy later revealed that the work had actually won by votes anyway, the most recognized by the jury.
Afterward, she spent a decade exhausting every option to re-enter Wrankwood, and slowly made some achievements of her own.
Of course, she changed her name as well... to Greta Stone.
So, Melanie. Now you know what an absolute fool I am.
I went out of my way to provoke fate into playing a joke on me.
Fortunately, that preposterous experience has taught me one thing: I should never doubt myself.
I will always believe in my own judgment, and never give up on anything I attempt.
Haha, you're more of a character than I took you for.
Though truth be told, I always thought that infallible persona of yours was just a front to intimidate others.
Come on, Greta.
So what's the sudden backstory for... You trying to win our sympathy?
Dead people in here and a Catastrophe out there... You think we'd still want to put our lives on the line, just for you? After all that's happened?
I know you still hold it against me, Abner.
...Ever since five years ago.
How could I? I mean, I was awarded Best Cinematography that year thanks to those classic shots from that film...
It's the highest honor any cinematographer could ever dream of... and you gave it to me!

You hit the peak of your career, then fell into despair. In just six months no one dared put you behind their cameras.

Before you joined the crew for The Death of Lady Dupont, you even lost your decades-old partner at the casino, yes?
You hate that film from five years ago, you hate the shot that won you the award, and you hate me for making you keep it—

My camera captured that Laura girl's fall, and when I saw how you reacted, I knew then and there that you had zero good intentions.
I warned you again and again, but you insisted on keeping that shot in the final cut...
The more I think back to how the jury commented on the shot's realism and shock value, the more I want to laugh!
Film is the art of creating dreams. Cameras are the eyes that see dreams, and the hands that turn fantasy into reality. But you insulted that girl's memory. You insulted me!
Now I hate this damn thing I'm holding, and I hate my work! You get it now?
If you really think so, then put it down, Abner.
What?
I'm sorry, we've derailed. Now to get back on topic—
After confirming you've received your compensation, any of you may leave this production at any time.
Would you rather flee this dangerous plate? By all means. Scared of the serial killer, and wish to find somewhere to hide? Go ahead.
Feel like playing detective for whatever reason? Knock yourself out. Even if you're the murderer yourself, and unwilling to give up on your vengeance, you have my blessing.
We've already filmed most of The Death of Lady Dupont, with only a few climactic scenes to cover in the last two days of our schedule.

Melanie?
You do realize I can hardly find a last minute stand-in for Lady Dupont herself, yes?
You know I don't have a choice.
I'm still counting on this film to beat Marianne.
Even a Catastrophe cloud can have a silver lining.
Marianne retired from cinema years ago. I've never understood why you're so obsessed with her.
That's none of your business.
But let me be frank: you won't get to demand special treatment as the female lead or insist on those 'rules of the film set' you love so much, once everyone else has evacuated.
Haha.
Steven.
I know, I know! I'll fix the ending by tonight!
I believe in my decision to invest in this story, darling, and I believe you've collected enough inspiration over the past few days.
Catastrophe draws near, and death is right before our eyes... Surely your god loves material like that?
Alright, thank you for your time, people, and I wish you well on your future endeavors.
That's all from me.
Old Dalton's death and Shelly freezing in the cold storage were no accidents...
Now Michael and Marion are dead too, in such a horrible way...
Who's behind all this? Why all the murders in the first place? And why are they so... gruesome?
I have a feeling that Michael saw this coming.
He looked a lot more serious than usual when we met the night before the murder.
He must've figured out the real reason Dalton died, panicked, and decided to get out of here, but...
Now we'll never know what he was hiding.
Maybe this all has to do with that accident five years ago, but what's really worrying me is that...
The killer's not even trying to make them look like accidents anymore. What if they're trying to tell us—
That until the entire truth is brought to light, nobody is safe.
Omigosh, sorry Molly, did my babbling scare you?
Don't worry, it doesn't matter if there really is, like, a crazy killer running out there, I'll protect you no matter how many of them there are.
Oh, that's not it, Detective Tippi. I was just thinking about... what I can do on my end.
Huh?
Michael's dead now, but that doesn't mean what he wanted me to do doesn't matter anymore.
The killer might be planning on using the escape landboats Miss Stone mentioned for their next move...
So if we can figure another way out, or put together something that'll get us to the ground, or even just find an exhaust vent...
We might be able to get away from this maniac hiding in the shadows and save ourselves during the climax.
Molly...
Tippi, you were the first person to really open up to me after I joined the crew.
Even though I kept messing up and getting you into trouble, you were always there to stand up for me. When Marion would forget my name, you were always ready to give her a proper reminder.
You're always looking after me, comforting me, protecting me...
So this time, I want to protect you too.
The Liberi gives a gentle hug to her most precious friend in the whole crew.
Once this is all over, we're totally gonna have a movie marathon at your place. Twenty-four hours of non-stop screams, paired with our favorite drinks and fried finballs.
I wanna see what the house you grew up in looks like, and meet that sister you're always bragging about.
...Sure.
I'm terribly sorry to interrupt your little sorority huddle.
Oh, wow, Miss Rutherford? What brings you to the prop room? Where's your assistant?
Didn't you receive Greta's notice on your terminals? She's already sent out compensation to everyone and officially disbanded the crew.
Whether you're scared of the killer, worried about the Catastrophe, or want to stay and finish the shoot, we're all free to do as we please.
So naturally it only took a blink of an eye for that spineless assistant of mine to run off god-knows-where.
Do as we please?
Don't tell me Miss Stone—no, none of the crew even care about the real reason all these murders are happening?
Frankly, my dear, I don't give a damn, and I don't see why you should either. Surely the reasonable thing to do is escape and leave that to the police?
Or, as Greta said, you can leave the truth to whoever feels like playing detective.
So, why'd you come looking for us, ma'am?
I'm only here for you, Tippi, the background painter.
Me?
I admire Greta's gumption in wanting to film a Catastrophe, and this is a fine opportunity for me as well, but films cannot rely solely on natural phenomena.
I know you're more passionate about cinema than most, and you must have heard how important this project is to me.
So I implore you, from the bottom of my heart, to put your talent in ambience-building and color-matching to work, and paint the perfect backdrop for my grand finale.
Wow...
I mean, I'd totally love to, but...
Go help her out, Tippi.
Hey, Molly, what's with the raincoat? Are you really going 'exploring'?
Mhm.
Don't worry, I've been part of the prop team for ages now, and I know my way around the plate. I'll be fine.
Remember what we were just talking about? This is something I need to do.
Alright then.
Once I'm done helping out with the backdrops, I'll come find you, promise!
You may enter, Madam Stone.
You know it was me before I even opened the door?
Yes. I have been waiting for you.
Your little investigation the other day forced me to reveal my plan of filming a real Catastrophe to the whole crew.
The timing couldn't have been worse. Abner and the rest of the crew were already unhappy with me. So I had to make some last-minute decisions. Big decisions.
If I wanted to, I could sue you for damages incurred by your act of industrial sabotage, Lucian.
But?
But I'm not going to punish my cast just to vent or assert my authority, especially when such actions would only complicate the situation.
And then, more murders. The killer even stopped hiding their savagery, and everyone's losing their minds, except you. You're the same as ever, cool as a cucumber, watching the crowd from a distance.
I suppose I can thank this crisis for making it clear that you have other reasons to be here.
But none of that's important right now. The crew's been disbanded. You can leave, or stay until the date specified in the contract. It's up to you.
Your part in this is done anyway.
Hardly.
The moment you came to find me, you proved that my part in this is not yet done.
...I can't magic up another Lady Dupont, or bring the three Rockefeller brothers back to life.
We're running out of both time and manpower. So I need to figure out the best use of our limited resources.
Lucian, I'd like you to be the lead for the rest of the film.
In the middle of the night, the clackety-clack of typing echoes through the office.
A despondent Perro curls up in front of his typewriter. He glances at his latest manuscript, and stubs a cigarette out on it.
Ending, ending, ending...
Lady Dupont needs to die in the ending...
'After she elopes with the second Rockefeller, they vanish from high society and live a poetic life out in the countryside.'
'But Rockefeller's life of affluence has left too much of a mark on him. He eventually shames Lady Dupont for her empty family name and leaves her. In despair, she slits her own throat...'
Too realistic...
And do they really have to be punished after leaving their extravagant lives behind?
This takes place back when Columbia held wealth as its highest virtue. I'm just trying to recreate it and let the audience judge for themselves!
The little cigarette burn grows, spreads, and swallows the whole paper up. Steven takes a deep breath and blows away the ashes.
'In the end, Lady Dupont chooses none of the Rockefellers. She had only wanted to escape her false destiny through marriage, but underestimated just how toxic love can be.'
'The brothers, unbeknownst to each other, each plot their own revenge, and Lady Dupont is killed by three different schemes at the same time.'
No, too lowbrow...
Getting stuck in a quagmire of love even after not choosing anyone makes it sound like it's fate at work, but it's really just slapping in a trope to patch up the gaps.
The final shot of her body being pulverized into nothing would definitely be shocking, but the setup's just too crude.

'The Dupont's family's false claims of being descended from Victorian nobility are finally exposed to the public. No matter how deep the Rockefeller brothers' love for her is, they cannot shield her from punishment.'
'The only time in her life that Lady Dupont is treated as true nobility is when the real Dupont family demands the death penalty for her...'
It's too... neat!
All tied up with a bow to show off how deep and meaningful it is, but it lacks any of the madness or romance a film called The Death of Lady Dupont should have!
Tch, out of draft paper. Let me fetch some more from the filing cabinet...
Whoa!
Steven slips on a wad of paper he threw to the ground earlier, falling flat on his back.
Only now does he notice that the entire office is filled with discarded drafts. There is nowhere left for him to stand.
He lies down amidst his aborted ideas, covering his face with his hands as he yells in frustration.
Get a Kubricking grip, Steven Quay! You've been messing with the ending for two whole months now, and don't even have a shitty half-baked scene to show for it!
This is your baby! You wrote every damn line in this story! Don't tell me you don't even know what you want to write?!
If you need to suffer to make a classic, then this must be a goddamn masterpiece, right?

You've got the time pressure of the shooting, the shock of hearing about filming a Catastrophe, the fear of some maniac going around killing the crew... You got it all, man! What more could you want?!
'God of Inspiration', my ass!
That was just some stupid excuse to get Greta off your back. Your god ain't coming—you lost his Falking contact details!
Who's there...?

I've come up with a few initial concepts for Lady Dupont's finale.

Director Quay's not even done fixing up the script yet, and you've already come up with so many ideas for the ending!

That lad's certainly taking his sweet time with it, yes. I believe that, both as the leading lady of The Death of Lady Dupont and its investor, I have an obligation to take responsibility for this film.
As I said before, the revival of my career in Wrankwood will begin once Lady Dupont gets a good, proper death.
Lupukarn Holiday, The Portrait of Erin Daz, The Bridges of County March... These mood reference shots are all taken from films starring Marianne Blake, aren't they?
Do you... like her work?
She retired long before you were born.
Er, I mean, like, my dad really loves her films! Besides, the classics are classics for a reason, right?
Both her acting chops and taste in projects were impeccable.
It's... kinda surprising to hear you say that. I could've sworn you hated her. Everyone's been comparing you guys for years.
Indeed. All those years...
Congratulations on winning your eighth Best Actress award, Miss Blake!
Ever since you started your career, you've been at least nominated for an award practically every year. The industry sees you as an insurmountable mountain for fresh new talent to try and climb.
Are there any particular up-and-comers you think we should keep an eye on?
I appreciate the flattery, but I'm hardly an 'insurmountable mountain'. I'm still climbing my way up myself.
There are always plenty of new faces streaming into Wrankwood, brimming with talent, and it's hard for me to pick any specific name—
How about Melanie Rutherford?
I tip my hat to you, Miss Blake.
Today I received the award for Best Supporting Actress, two years slower than it took you.
I've seen all your films. As our reporter friend here said, you really are a natural-born performer, the tallest peak in Wrankwood... but only for now.
Give me some time, and I will overcome you.
Well, I'm looking forward to it.
Wow, Melanie and Marianne are really duking it out this scene. Time to get out the popcorn.
They've been rivals for a while now, haven't they? It's like they're always racing to get the most awards. So why'd they agree to be in the same project?
Shh! Did you forget everything they taught you when you joined the crew, you rude little shit? Go apologize to Miss Rutherford right now!
I-I'm really sorry!
Let's not be too harsh on the fresh meat, Melanie.
'Our films are built upon rules.' You've been in the industry longer than I have, Miss Blake. You must have experienced why they're so important firsthand.
