Which reminds me, I still haven't congratulated you for that Best Actress award you got at Leithanien's Spire Film Festival a few days ago.
That was just a small award ceremony. Meanwhile, your latest film swept every single major award in Wrankwood, again.
I still haven't truly overcome you, Miss Blake.
I would like to thank everyone for coming to this press conference regarding The Eternally Shining Star.
We appreciate your care and concern during this difficult time. The rumors are true: our film crew really did run into a Catastrophe during our shoot. I was injured, and am still recovering now.
Thankfully, we didn't return empty handed. We managed to hold onto some of our precious, precious footage.
But I believe this is Heaven working in mysterious ways. It's trying to tell me that.... it's time for me to bid farewell to the silver screen.
As both the lead actress of The Eternally Shining Star and its investor, I am hereby announcing that neither the film nor my performance will be submitted for nomination for any awards.
It will remain as one last gift from Marianne Blake to her cinephile friends, and nothing more.
I won't be leaving Wrankwood immediately, of course. Just moving into more of a behind-the-scenes role for now.
But I doubt I'll be content with that for long. I'm always eager to seek new opportunities, to start a new chapter of my life as 'Jara Booker Wilson'.
Oh, you're here too, Miss Rutherford. Could I request a quick interview?
Your latest film will be releasing soon, and most were predicting that only you and Miss Blake would be in the running for this year's Best Actress at the Wrankwood Awards.
So how do you feel about the announcement of her sudden retirement, and the withdrawal of her new movie from the nominations?
Does this mean...
I won?
After Jara—err, Miss Blake retired from acting, you got all those roles as brand spokeswoman and lead actress, and even got invited to be on the judging panels for the big film festivals.
But not long after, you suddenly left it all behind because... some personal reasons. And when you came back last time—
Nothing was the same anymore.
Her retirement gave me quite the shock, but I still believed I had won fairly, through my own talent.
I believed that I, too, could become an 'insurmountable mountain' and a legend of the silver screen. One that outshone even her.
But in the end, Wrankwood's Best Actress Melanie Rutherford let it all go to her head, swayed by the endless praise of the critics, the flattery of the sponsors, and the applause of the audience.
Wealth and fame, alcohol and scandal... I lived an... indecent life, those few years.
Yeah, tell me about it. Those tabloids have, like, zero respect for your privacy!
But once I met you in person, I could tell. Maybe you've made a few mistakes here and there, but your love for acting's the real deal.
No need to console me, little Liberi. No one's more acutely aware of my failings than I am.
Marianne could switch from a hardened soldier to a poor servant girl and back again at the drop of a hat. A single dress rehearsal was all she needed to figure out the ideal lighting and positions.
But if I were ever to take a single day off, spend one hour less on studying the script, or arrive at the rehearsal fifteen minutes late, my performance would fall into the gutter.
A mere two years of living in indulgence was all it took for me to lose the skills I once picked up with ease. I disappointed both the directors and the fans.
I had never realized just how many unending waves of new talent were looking up to me. I had never realized what the position in entertainment Marianne had fought so hard to defend actually meant.
I thought I could afford to be like her, and enjoy the double delights of cinema and life. I thought there'd be no harm in having a little fun after all those chasing and competing.
It was only then that I finally realized Marianne Blake was a truly infuriating genius... In every sense of the term.
With her final film, and that chance Catastrophe... Marianne received a revelation to retire from cinema, and all I could do was sigh. Even unknowable Mother Nature was on her side.
But now that you're a lead actress again, you've got the chance to win back everyone's trust!
I mean, you're even getting to film alongside a Catastrophe too!
You're right. I've spent more than a decade piecing my self-confidence and acting skills back together. I could very well be but a single step away from the top again.
Greta's offer to give me the part of Lady Dupont couldn't have come at a better time, and now, even her grand ambitions have left me a special little 'gift'.
Come to think of it, the Lady is a rather fitting role for me.
Surely she knows her brother is lying to her? Surely she has doubted her last name once or twice?
But no, she unconsciously clings to her glamorous life, refusing to accept the mediocrity of her birth. She prays desperately that she is special, that she is talented.
I wonder, what sort of ending will she face, amidst the storm?
Miss Rutherford.
Yes?
We should watch some horror flicks together!
Horror flicks...?
If you want to climb your way back to the top, you can't limit yourself to copying Miss Blake, right? Like, you've always had pretty different styles of acting.
We're gonna put together a finale more powerful than the one in Lupukarn Holiday, and Lady Dupont's name will go down in cinema history!
Ha. Very well then.
Also...
I'm sure you know what happened between you and Miss Blake better than I do, but I bet she meant it when she said you'd surpass 'Marianne Blake' one day.
She might still think so, even now.
You might be right.
You, you're... Are you real, or just a figment of my imagination? Am I really being visited by the god of inspiration?
Don't tell me I've gone crazy from working on this script...
Madness can transform ink on the page into sweet fruit, or allow a stiff mind to wander freely across the wilderness of time and space.

You cower in the corner of a dark room, forced to chase a deadline even after witnessing a string of brutal murders. Yet, for all your soul-searching and brain-racking, you have come up with only three endings for Lady Dupont.

I promised Greta I'd have an ending that'd impress everyone by tonight. The only people left are those insane enough to risk their lives for cinema, and they're waiting for me.
But they bet on the wrong man.
The realm of art only accepts natural-born geniuses, not some dried-up piece of crap slaving away at a typewriter!
The Death of Lady Dupont... You already have the most captivating ending for this story just from its title.
While the shocking brilliance of her death will certainly fade after the film is copied reel after reel...
But Wrankwood, after all, is a factory hawking such fantasies all across Terra, is it not?
You are a nation of liars, fooling even yourselves. Even the Columbian dream was born from a promotional film for the Pioneer project, after all.

Just to be clear, I care for neither political metaphors nor realities, only that you have deeply misunderstood the essence of performance. Drama can change lives, but it would never use such a... mechanical way.
But by setting death as Lady Dupont's ending, you show you still possess an intuition for drama.
Intuition?

When I was a student, I hated dragging death into the stories I wrote.
I thought of it as a cheap trick, only good for plugging up holes in the plot or squeezing the right emotions out of the audience.
Actually talented writers could give a film a proper climax without it.
Then what changed you? A shocking memory, or perhaps some phantom pain you cannot recall?
I had the same problem five years ago when I was working with Greta on another project.
She didn't mince words: she told me the back half of the script was too dull, and audiences would be falling asleep. If I didn't change it, it'd bomb at the box office.
So I shut myself in my office just like I do now, pushing my mind to the brink of collapse but getting nothing out of it...
And then, Laura fell and died.
An opportunity straight from Heaven, the perfect plot device.
Just as Greta expected, the rest of the film was panned by critics. But that scene alone became an overnight sensation, to the point that...
You started to see death as more than a cheap trick. Rather, as the most sincere form of expression, brimming with artistry.
Yeah...
Seeing how you've had that sudden realization, all that has happened on this plate should provide you with nourishment.

A death while shooting, a deadly hallucination, bodies hanging from beams... With all this to inspire you, why not write them into your story, and let the same drama be performed both on and off the screen?
Because they're still not good enough.
Then what about turning away from those who have already exhausted their possibilities?
Wh-What do you mean?
Who else in your story can ignite a spark in Lady Dupont's bosom? Who is distant enough from all the grudges and resentments, yet still able to bring about the most drama—
As well as the best of performances?
The Messenger... It's the Messenger who brought the bad news! He's the very trigger for Lady Dupont's new fate!
Vengeance or repentance? Lady Dupont has a new choice to make, and at the same time, the Messenger must contend with the consequences of intervening in another's destiny...
This is it! This is the ending I wanted!
Lucian... Yes... I'll have Lucian be the star of the final scene...
You want me to become the lead actor...
That's right.
I can't say I understand you, but I have a feeling that even if I hadn't approached you, you would've come to me anyway.
You have the most potential of any actor I've ever seen.
The Messenger doesn't have much screentime, but your performance added a complex and delicate core to a role that was originally a nothingburger.
And I'm sure that whatever your goal here is, you won't be leaving before the shoot ends.
If so, then why not do me a favor and help me out with this?
I'll give you time to think it over, of course. So far, it's just a thought I had, and I haven't spoken to Steven about it yet.
He promised me the finished script by tonight, but honestly, I'm not optimistic.
If only that god of his knocked on his door.
I see... Changing the focus to the Messenger was the only way to steer the plot towards brilliance!
I can already imagine the shock on everyone's faces when they read the new ending. Well, once I'm done with it.
But...
I see you've added a lot of direction notes for his lines. Most of them are... quite detailed.
While you were giving me advice on my writing just now, you mentioned the quirks of Lucian's performances, and where he likes to put pauses in dialogue.
You even told me which angle suits him best for close-ups to capture his most subtle expressions, and what kind of lighting best highlights his unique character...
It's like you've known him for a long, long time. Maybe even taught him—
So how did you know all these things about Lucian?
You called for me, Troupe Leader?
Come with me, Playwright.
The final act is underway, and as always, the entirety of the audience is enraptured by the 'performance' on-stage.
It's rare for Whiteflower to return to the stage. Will you not toast to her once the performance is over?
What of Blood Diamond and Abyss?
They are merely supporting roles this time. All their scenes have already concluded.

The rotting stage mechanism has spread the blood of the 'magician's assistant' and the chosen 'lucky audience member' all over the stage. Their corpses have been stuffed into prop boxes, set afloat on the river to disappear forevermore...
The magician herself completes her final show as she treads on a stage yet slick with blood.
Blood Diamond and Abyss should have chiseled their way out of the boxes by now, and be on their way back to the stage for the curtain call alongside Whiteflower the magician.
Should they, now?
And here I was, thinking those two children have turned performance into reality, disappearing forevermore...
As I recall, this was a new scene you added half a year ago while revising the script... During a night you claimed to have had a revelation?
Leader...
Come with me, Playwright.
The show is far from over.
He is my favorite leading man.
He once left me, but in the end, we shall be reunited.
A petite Zalak gropes around in the darkness as she traverses the pitch-black plate layer.
Hmm... I see how it is.
The structure and systems look complex, but once you figure out the theory behind it...
Even a prop-maker can operate it.
For example, here, adjust its direction first...
Then set a time, pull down the lever, open the gate, and...
Done.
Good, just like I thought.
This way, I'll have nothing to worry about even if something unexpected happens.
Now, there's just one last thing to do...
I need to go find her.
I'm sure you have reservations, Lucian.
The plate's in a crisis, and everybody's in danger. I wouldn't trust a cold-hearted producer who is demanding the shoot go on either.
We do not need to trust each other.
Heh. You're not wrong.
In my twenty years in Wrankwood, I've never trusted a single person I've worked with. I trust only in my own judgment.
I invited Melanie right when she most needed an opportunity. I saw right away at that auction that you were an acting genius. And I was sure Abner would join the crew even though he hates my guts.
...I even still believe that Laura's death five years ago was the only way to save that movie.
You had nothing to do with the accident, but you still made use of her death.
She was a talented rookie. Eager, hardworking, and full of passion for cinema. It's just that sometimes, that enthusiasm got to her head, and made her break a few rules.
Steven was still green back then, and the shoot wasn't going as planned. Halfway through the project, I knew that film would burn through all my assets and leave my reputation in tatters.
I needed an 'external factor' to help me solve that crisis.
Laura's death.
Using the media to hype up the accident would be too predictable. Those busybodies would just sigh and forget all about it the moment they moved on.
So I used a lot of money to squash the police investigation and media's attention, along with a healthy amount of hush money to Laura's family.
I got the editor to keep the footage of the fall that Abner captured on camera, and turned it into the climax of the film.
Even now, there are some people who still think she was just an acting genius who retired early.
You covered up her death.
I immortalized her in film.
Someone will eventually notice what you did.
Every year, on the day of her sacrifice, I go visit her. Five years, and I'm the only one who brings flowers to her grave.
Do you not feel guilt?
I'm certainly to blame for it. But now's not the time for repentance.
Right now, there's only one thing on my mind: finishing this film.
Once The Death of Lady Dupont is screening, the murders on set are bound to get the media's attention. I'll take that chance to officially release the truth behind Laura's death.
When that time comes, there will be a lot more people grieving for her. And that's when I'll bear the blame.
I hope you realize that I didn't confess all that to you on a whim.
You want to use the truth to make a deal.
When I first met you, I thought it was fate throwing me a bone, dropping a big present on my lap before shooting started.
But it was your behavior after you joined the crew that proved to me you are no ordinary man.
Dalton died right in front of you, but you kept your cool. You even looked forward to future filming.
After you discovered my plans to film a Catastrophe, you took the perfect opportunity to leak those plans to the camera assistant most likely to spill them.
And now with the crew officially dismissed, almost everyone is feeling desperate. Except you. You were waiting in your room for me to visit, as if you're no stranger to sudden death and tragedy.
Who are you exactly, and why did you agree to join my film crew?
I was enticed by your script. No more and no less.
Sure.
The way things are now, interrogating you about your history isn't going to help my shoot, but I was hoping you would repay my honesty in kind.
I still believe in my vision, Lucian. No matter what I find out about you, no matter how suspicious, I will never withdraw my invitation for you to be my male lead.
But—
If you really can't explain why you're here, if you've got your sights on something way over my head...
If you think Laura's death and everything it's caused five years down the line are the real 'story' being filmed on this plate, and you're here for that...
Then I, Greta Stone, would like to invite you again.
Please be our next 'leading man', Lucian.
If that is your wish... then it is mine as well, Madam Stone.

lines.st: '...Greta... sent out compensation to everyone and officially disbanded the crew.'
'Whether you're scared of the killer, worried about the Catastrophe, or want to stay and finish the shoot, we're all free to do as we please.'
Finish the shoot? Greta is still thinking about her movie right now?
She must have noticed the connection between the murders of the crew and the one five years ago.
So she's doing it on purpose...
'Frankly, my dear, I don't give a damn, and I don't see why you should either.'
'Or, as Greta said, you can leave the truth to whoever feels like playing detective.'
The cast and crew both mercilessly bully newcomers. The producer even massaged a tragic and fatal fall into a scene that straddles the line between truth and lie.
If you will not repent for what happened five years ago, nor make your deeds known to the public...
Do you believe yourself innocent, Greta? Have you forgotten how you edited Laura into your film, all so she could die over and over again with every showing?

lines.x: A bulge on the bed is faintly outlined by the dim nightlight. The person beneath the blanket is fast asleep, ignorant of the judgment about to fall upon her.
Fast asleep? That desecrator of the dead, forgetter of sins, ignorer of warnings... How could Greta sleep so soundly?
Someone quietly creeps closer to the bed, pulling a razor-sharp dagger out of a sleeve. Slender fingers grip its hilt tightly, lifting the terrifying weapon high into the air.
I don't even want to hear your confession! I don't even want to hear your screams! Just go away already! You must PAY!
This feeling... It's not right...
The blanket is lifted up. There is nobody under it, aside from a black shadow.
A shadow? How can a shadow exist on its own?
The intruder steps back a bit, as the shadow slowly condenses into a familiar shape.
...Miss.Christine?
A trap...
So we finally meet.
I welcome you, my elusive guest.
Is it really... you?
Moira... The most dedicated member of the crew was actually a three-time murderer...
It should have been four.
When did you find out, Lucian?
The first to notice your abnormal behavior was Miss.Christine, not me.
In that very first scene after I joined the cast, she intruded upon the shoot.
She rarely approaches anyone other than me, but she approached you.
Since then, she has made contact with you many times.
Tippi believed she decided to accompany you as you watched movies in the prop room because she likes the both of you.
That is only half correct.
Miss.Christine was only ever interested in you.
Look. Even now, even as you hold that dreadful dagger, she still stands by your side.
Ridiculous...
Don't tell me that's your only reason to suspect me? Just because this mysterious creature is fond of me?
Or maybe she only does what you tell her to. She is your pet, after all...
Moira looks into Miss.Christine's deep, blue eyes.
The very next moment, she no longer has conscious control over herself.
Joy, sorrow, anger. All sorts of emotions roil through her heart, before finally settling into a blank void, one that terrifies anyone looking upon it.
You continue to misunderstand her.
As I have explained before, none may interfere with her intentions and actions.
I did not merely mean that she is free-spirited by nature, but rather, nobody has the 'ability' to interfere with her.
It is she who possesses the 'ability' to influence others.
The mysterious black creature nonchalantly licks her paws.
Moira finds it difficult to reconcile the Miss.Christine she sees now with the enormous pressure she felt earlier.
Miss.Christine is extremely sensitive to emotions. No carefully buried pain or flicker of malice escapes her notice.
Over countless years, she has wandered across all of Terra, tasting the entire spectrum of feeling.
Rather than any particular flavor, she prefers a blend.
And her judgment is derived from instinct rather than logic. It does not err.
Solving a murder without even discovering a motive. Should I congratulate you?
Vengeance.
For who?
For that ghost entangled in the minds of the majority of the crew.
Madam Stone, the one who stands before you now is a relative of that rookie actress Laura, who fell to her death five years ago.
One who loved her dearly. Her little sister.
So, out of all the choices Miss Stone gave us after disbanding the crew, you chose to play detective, Lucian.
No need to bluff. I know you're not afraid of me exposing you.
If it would let more people know the truth behind Laura's death and the conduct of the film crew, you would have gladly exposed yourself, given the right opportunity.
...But not right now.
It's over, Moira.
You hid yourself well. A simple identity and an innocent facade were the ideal cover for you.
During nearly all of the murders, you were with Tippi. That painter trusts you deeply, and was always happy to provide you an alibi... even as you left your meetings partway through.
Looking back, every 'accident' has involved you in some way.

Dalton's death was caused by a prop knife being switched, which led to the former prop master being dismissed. You, naturally, took over his work, which afforded you even greater opportunities for crime.
As for the second 'accident'...
When Shelly walked into cold storage.
No, she had intended to kill her before that, but failed.
Do you remember how Shelly had a hallucination and interrupted the shoot, Madam Stone?
While she was rooted in place, unable to move, a heavy container fell from above.
You saved her. Moira single-handedly held onto the rope, preventing the two of you from being harmed...
But it was not out of the kindness of her heart.
At the time, everyone was focused on Shelly's antics. Very few people noticed the dangling container—
Let alone rushed to the other end of the rope.
Right, only the one who tampered with the rope in the first place could have reacted that quickly...
The failed attempt revealed to all that Shelly suffered from hallucinations, which you took advantage of.
I knew her deepest nightmares.
She mistook me for my sister Laura, and babbled all her sinful secrets to me.
But even to the very end, she never repented. Instead, she began to blame Laura for crossing the line... She had to die.
As for Marion and Michael...
Michael only ever treated me as an innocent little errand girl. He never figured out I was after his life, even entrusting me with the important matter of sneaking out of the plate.
And Marion? She never felt the slightest guilt for what happened five years ago. She HAD to die!
Escape? Nobody can escape!
Do you know just how much suffering and malice my sister had to endure? Yet all she ever talked about was how much experience she was getting, and how happy she was to be part of the cast.
Whenever I was worried I wouldn't make it as a prop-maker, whenever I silently shed tears as the crew ostracized me... she would use herself as an example to comfort me, to encourage me...
But it was only after she died that I learned what that 'experience' you gave her really was!
How dare you look at me like that, Greta? Are you doubting what I just said?

Surely you haven't forgotten how you bribed the police and the media and suppressed the news of Laura's death. You even turned her accident into a mockery of the truth in a movie! How could she possibly rest in peace after that?!
No, Moira.
Madam Stone has not forgotten her deeds. Rather, she is doubting your identity.
My identity? A prop-maker, an unremarkable Zalak, an avenger of crimes unanswered—
Laura had no younger sister.
She was never a part of your life.
Wha... What are you saying?
What kind of interrogation is this?
Five years ago, after the accident, I gave Laura's family compensation.
I even hired people to scour her entire family history and their circumstances, all so I could guarantee that none of them would leak the truth, not even her most distant relatives.
After Lucian told me the truth he had deduced, I was still in disbelief, so I pulled out the list of Laura's family members and checked it again...
I regret to inform you... There was no 'Moira' on that list.
...You're lying!
I can explain everything that has happened to you, but you will need time to accept it.
So put down the dagger, Moira.
It should not be yours.
Put it down...?
It's far too late for that.
What was that?!
What's with the sudden earthquake?! Somebody save me!
Did the plate hit something?
If I'd known this was coming, I'd have skedaddled out of this place already... *whimper*... What the hell is going on here...?
Crew members stream out of their rooms into the crowded corridor, none with the slightest idea what was happening.
Lucian's shadow passes by a bevy of curses, complaints, laments, and prayers before arriving at the end of the corridor. He looks out the window.
Hey, guys, doesn't it feel like... the plate is speeding up?!
Moira locks the door.
She does not know if the man named Lucian will catch up to her, or how long it would take.
The room shakes violently, letting her know that the sabotage she performed had taken effect—

The cockpit is destroyed, the engine's output is locked to its highest setting, and its route is set. The studio plate speeds up as it approaches the nearby Catastrophe... and shows no signs of stopping.
In a few hours, they will find themselves with the misfortune of facing a storm... No, a trial.
The vast majority of this cast and crew had to answer for the accident five years ago...
For the death of her sister.
Laura had no younger sister.
She was never a part of your life.
Wh-What was he talking about...
Moira mumbles to herself incessantly. She sifts through the piles of junk in the prop room, and finds an old VCR. She sets it up, and pulls out a videotape from one of her inner pockets.
A videotape she has watched countless times.
She presses down on the play button.
The smile of a young woman appears on the screen.
...Laura.
...My sister. You're real... tell me you're real...
I know whenever you laughed, there was a dimple in only your left cheek. You had big eyes with light green irises, that were always so gentle when you looked at me.

You could peel an entire apple without breaking its skin in thirty seconds. Whenever you were happy, you'd whistle a big strong 'phweet'. You'd use Arts to make small ice crystals to use as hair clips...
You told me about your life in Wrankwood, how your very first role got edited down to only three seconds of screentime in the final cut.
You told me about how much you struggled just to get a few more scenes. While a few assholes talked about you behind your back, most of the crew still looked after you.

You told me how, even though you came from a small town where you couldn't even buy a VCR, even though you had to learn a lot of things from scratch, even though opportunities that are easy for others to get are far out of reach for us...
We still shouldn't be discouraged.

You never mentioned how much bullying you had to suffer through, how much pain you endured. You would go on and on about how beautiful the sunrise in Wrankwood was, looking towards the future with such firm eyes.
You also had times when you were sad, even though you only ever mentioned how homesick you felt at night, when all was quiet and the only ones kept you company were the twin moons low in the sky.
You told me how alike we were, and how you had my back and would support me forever.
Ever since we were little—
Moira suddenly stops.
She shakes her head like mad, trying to force more details out of her memory.
But she can say no more. She finds her memories of Laura have an unusually distinct cutoff point.
They had never run through the grass together, hand in hand. They had never fallen asleep in each other's arms as their parents sang to them.
As she trawls further back through her memories, every image to do with her sister is simply blank.
The videotape ends. The television screen goes dark.
Moira sees her own face reflected in it.
Tears silently stream down her cheek.
You were never a part of Laura's life...
Laura was never a part of yours...
But... But if that's really true...
Then what do you even have left, Moira?
...Who are you?
*sigh* First the serial murders, now this morning's mysterious explosion. The hell's going on here...
Why's the wind so strong?
Oh, right, according to the schedule, today's the day we're filming the Catastrophe.
Hautin stands in the street of the exterior set. Thanks to the nearby storm, the once-colorful background walls are covered in a layer of dust.
She never expected to experience so many twists and turns after joining the film crew. She's only ever thought about making movies. As for actually being in one... She can't stand the idea of it.
That's... the Catastrophe Miss Stone wanted to film...
Earlier, the Catastrophe everyone was making such a fuss about had simply been a faint gray dot out in the wastes.
Now, it has condensed into a storm that blots out the sky, a storm that refuses to allow onlookers to tear their gazes away from it.
Small-scale spirals of wind formed from Originium dust circle around the plate, visible to the naked eye.
...I gotta say, it really is a s-spectacular sight... If you saw a scene like this in a movie theater...
Please, Terra bless us. I've had such bad luck, please just let us finish filming safely.
No... Who cares about the shoot—a safe return to central Wrankwood is good enough for me! Please!
The plate responds to Hautin's prayer with an even-more-unusual-than-earlier sound and shaking.
Whoa! What the Hitchcock was that?!
Ahh—!
Hautin has wondered many times before: If she were actually in the middle of a Catastrophe, what would she hear?
The howling of the wind? The roaring of explosions? The shrieking of the panicked crowd? Turns out it is none of those.
It is the sound of walls and metal structures being squashed as flat as an empty can, one after the other.
It is a sound that instills extreme terror in oh-so-fragile humans, who begin to worry their organs, bones, and blood vessels will suffer a similar fate.
The turbulent sky licks against the edge of the plate, tearing sets that once starred in classic movies to shreds with the wind, before flinging them out into the wasteland.
Hautin moves aside the scaffolding that had fallen on her, but finds her legs too weak to muster the strength to move.
She looks at the devastation in a daze, barely able to believe her eyes.
My God... How did we already get so close to the Catastrophe?!
What the...?! THIS is Greta's plan for the shoot?!
The Originium storm's right in our faces! What are we, some sort of suicide squad?
No... no way... This has to be the killer's doing, right? Didn't you hear the explosion earlier?
Someone blew up the plate's cockpit... There's no way we can slow the plate down or change directions now...
Right now we're only barely in contact with the storm, but once we hit its core...
They're not gonna find even a single brick from this plate... *sob*...
Alright, enough!
So who on Terra was it? Their revenge wasn't enough for them, so now they're gonna take out the rest of us too?!
The... murderer is Moira of the prop team...
What? Who's talking?
I said! The killer responsible for the three murders is Moira!
Moira...? You're kidding me... That unremarkable Zalak?
Miss Stone caught her red-handed!
If Lucian weren't there, she would've become the next victim!
...So where's the little rat now? I'll go find her myself!
And what's the point of confronting a psycho killer when you can't even save yourself?!
She owes us an explanation! What if she gets innocents killed?!
You think I haven't thought of that?!
I already switched the wireless comms to broadcast mode to ensure I survive this. I'm sure everyone outside the plate has heard the truth by now.
The killer's trapped like a rat.
I have to live, I have to... Once I get back to central Wrankwood, all the bombshells I've collected these past few days will be enough to buy me a villa in Siesta—
Shit! The plate's tilting towards the Originium storm again!
Right, the landboats! We can use them to escape!
...They're gone.
What?
I went over to the emergency escape cabin earlier, and they were already wrecked by the storm... every last one of them...
There's no way out for us now...
...If I'm going to die anyway, I might as well take the chance and jump off the plate... I...
Cut that shit out!
Just move! Anywhere's safer than the film set! Let's go hide underground in the engine layer!
There truly are so many things to learn from the set design of a horror film... Thank you, Tippi.
No problem!
I have to get back and start making the visualizations for most of the visual effects first, then we'll finalize the details after Director Quay is done with the script.
Like I promised, I'm gonna design you a finale that'll leave Lady Dupont in the cinema history!
Right, I'm looking forward to it.
(Wait, how long have we talking about this? Did Molly ever find a way to escape—)
Ahh—!
Wh-What now?
That's not good.
Wait, there's a voice from the comms...
Catastrophe... *bzzt*... control cabin... destroyed...
Why's the signal so bad? It sounded like Abner's assistant. What kinda trouble is he getting into now—
The control cabin... has been destroyed...
The plate... is rushing towards the Catastrophe...
...killer... is Moira...
Tippi grips the wireless walkie-talkie and turns the volume knob as high as she can, but it stays silent.
She must have misheard, she thinks.
But if so, why is Miss Rutherford looking at her with such a sullen expression?
No... There's no way...
Tippi... I can't imagine anyone would joke about that in these circumstances.
But... When would she have even had the opportunity?
The nights when Shelly, Michael, and Marion died, she was watching movies with me the whole time, there wasn't—
Was there really no opportunity?
Tippi doubts her own mind.
Was Molly really right next to her the whole time during their movie nights?
No...
We always watched multiple movies back to back, so sometimes she'd leave when I was switching out the tape...
Sometimes she'd say Miss Stone was looking for her, sometimes it was to pick up something she forgot on the film set...
I'm sure you know how she's like, ma'am. She's still new to being the prop master and under, like, a ton of pressure, but she's super committed to her work...
Darling...
No, no, that only explains how she could've done it! It doesn't prove that she did...
A motive! Why would she kill all those people she had nothing to do with? Why don't I know anything?
Darling, maybe you didn't know your friend as well as you thought you did.
Let's just focus on the movie... so stop looking away from the screen!
Sorry, there's just been so much going on with the film crew lately, I'm... I'm a little nervous...
Don't worry, I'm here for you, girl!
No matter who I am, one thing's for sure: I'm your best friend on this crew.
Or maybe... maybe she never even thought of me as a friend...
Maybe she was just... using me...
The Liberi girl's head sinks.
She speaks no more.
Melanie hugs the little Liberi in front of her.
The girl who was consoling her just a few hours ago is now crying on her shoulder.
Melanie pats her back. She asks no questions, nor does she say anything about the price of growing up. She only hugs her.
Let's just leave this place first, Tippi.
The convoluted structure here will only put us in danger if the plate gets too close to the Catastrophe core.
We would be safer underground, though that too may be futile if the plate doesn't change direction...
Underground...
Over Melanie's shoulders, Tippi spots some of the material they salvaged from the horror film earlier.
'Underground'—The word circles around her brain.
Wait, I got it!
What? You've recovered your pep already?
No, I figured out how to change the plate's trajectory!
There was once a crew that snuck into another Wrankwood plate to film secretly—to save on rental costs—and they ended up with a super cool scary movie...
I re-watched it recently.
But that plate and the one we're standing on both came from the same batch made by Wrankwood...
So basically, the specs should be pretty much the same.
If my hunch is right... there should be another control cabin at the bottom of the engine layer.
...What are our odds of making it there?
To be honest... no idea.
But never tell me the odds. I gotta try.
There's no point mulling over something I can't answer on my own. Only when we're out of this crisis...
Will I be able to get the truth from her.
(Gentle singing♪)
(Cheerful whistling♪)
So this is where you were, Lucian.
That was my sister's favorite song. On breaks between shooting she'd often look to the sky and hum the tune.
I thought you'd already woken up.
I did.
Thanks to you. You showed me the truth I was blind to... and how cruel they truly were.
Then what are you doing now?
A rumbling comes from beneath their feet. The Wrankwood plate rushes towards the Catastrophe as the crew panics. No one would think to enter the prop room now, when they should be escaping.
But it is at this very moment that the prop master gets to work.
She goes around the room, fiddling with one mannequin after another, dressing them in clothes, correcting their postures... Her earnest focus is fully on them.
I only just remembered how I recognized Laura.
If you will allow me a guess... you met someone.
Yes, a very gentle Feline.
The weather was terrible that day. I was sitting on a bench for a long time, and there was water on my face—probably rain, but maybe tears...
The accident was caused by the negligence of the person in charge, but in the end I was the one who had to pay compensation and was kicked off the crew.
I didn't know anyone in Wrankwood, so I was no stranger to being treated like that.
I ran away from home, you know. After my parents died, there was nowhere else to go. So the only thing left for me was...
Death.
Then that person sat next to me. We didn't talk the entire time. He only gently waited for me to calm down...
But when he left, I realized a cassette tape was sitting on my knee.
That man brought Laura into my life, at a time when there was only darkness.
She became my sister. She protected me, encouraged me, and helped me pull myself together...
Laura was already long dead by then.
Her background, experiences, and even appearance all resembled yours. The man gave you motivation when you were at your lowest...
As well as anger, confusion, and a deep-rooted thirst for vengeance...
No doubt, that was no better than before.
Was that grief I saw flash through your eyes...? Or... pity? No, that's not it either...
More like... understanding, sympathy.
Did something similar happen to you?
Do you know him?
Of course.
He's an expert at grafting non-existent joy and anger onto your life, much like how an unscrupulous director adds extra elements as he pleases to a story he deems dull...
Oh, I suppose you would call it 'ad-libbing' in the film industry.
He pulls you from one pit of despair while guiding you into another, thus inciting the so-called beauty of drama.
...He's an old acquaintance, my teacher, the source of all my suffering, and a phantom that should be long dead.
Finding you, finding him...
Is the very reason I came here.
Moira, I'm sorry for denying all that you have.
No. It's not like that.
Moira finishes arranging her last mannequin, and returns to the center of the prop room.
She does a curtsy in Lucian's direction, and lightly taps her fingertips.
Lucian then realizes something has changed, and looks over to see that every mannequin has come 'alive'.
I don't think you've denied me.
In my worst hour, Laura appeared by my side, and has accompanied me for a long time now...
She's my sister, as I am hers.
I don't regret avenging her. I don't regret shouldering all these sins.
I will also be the last person in this land to remember her.
I cry, I laugh, I rage, I doubt, I hate, I grieve, I yearn...
And thus I have them, these intense feelings I would never have had otherwise.
I am alive.
The mannequins begin to move.
Lucian recognizes who they were modeled after... the deceased Dalton, Shelly, Michael, Marion, and also Moira herself.
Moira stretches out her hand, and holds her mannequins' hands one by one as they move past her.
No music except the trembling ground accompanies their movements, but the tiny Zalak prop master seems to have become the most passionate dancer on the land, as she embraces her myriad...
Strange, yet elegant.
I should be thanking you, Lucian. You helped me realize it.
When there was nothing left of 'Moira' anymore...
I felt as if I became a mirror, and that I could become—
Anyone!
Wake up.
Satisfaction born from despair is nothing but poison.
I've never been more awake in my entire life.
Would you also like to become my dance partner?
...Just like them.
As Moira finishes speaking, her dance partners throw themselves at Lucian—
(Gasping heavily)
Chill, Tippi, chill... deep breaths. Remember how the plot went in that movie—

...The heroine had her friend pick up a weapon and hide in a storage room to the left, while she herself ran the other way...
Storage room...
Alright, the route matches up!
Down we go... It's kinda dark in here... Well, dark is good... There should be a hidden door around the corner...
Straight ahead, and at the end of the corridor there should be a backup control cabin...
...She knew all the mannequins chasing down the squad were her friend's work. The whole squad pointed their fingers at her friend, and she was mad too...
Gotta get moving!
What are you doing here, Tippi?
I figured out how to change the plate's trajectory! We can avoid any more casualties, so let me through ASAP!
There's a way out of this...?
Don't listen to her lies! Isn't she the culprit's best friend?!
She could've been covering up for her this whole time! How else could we have missed all this happening right under our noses, right up until we're about to die?!
She tries not to think about her friend's betrayal, even though she still has things to say...
She ignores the crowd mocking her. They're only attacking her to protect themselves...
It... it was you, the one always hiding in the back! First you interrupt the shooting, now you're preventing us from going home!
She wants to save everyone, including her friend. She wants answers...
Stop her! Don't let her through!
Agh, my eyes—!
Tippi grabs the spray-gun from her belt and glares at the fearsome weapon-wielding crew.
You leave me no other choice...
Are you guys afraid of one girl? Get her!
The murders, the Catastrophe... The film crew was already on-edge from having to hide in the engine layer, so the spray-painter's behavior only rouses their pent-up anger.
Their self-defense weapons scrape the floor as everyone rushes towards Tippi.
Her hands are trembling. There is no way she can take them all alone, and she prays the hover unit on her back will work...
Her final trump card.
A black creature shows up at the corner of the corridor.
It is as if the noise drew her here, but also as if she is merely passing by.
No one notices her as they rush by, their heads filled with all sorts of extreme emotions.
Miss.Christine takes a sniff, and the rushing crowd slows down in her eyes... until they freeze in place.
Anger. Extremely spicy, but without a little sour envy to balance it out, it tastes a bit off...
Panic. Bitter and hard to keep down, but a little dash of sweet comfort will turn it into a nice dessert...
Grief. A salty mix of tears and sighs that brings unnecessary trouble if too much is added. Must be carefully paired with other flavors...
Pain. The most complex and ever-changing emotion. It used to be my favorite...
But after meeting Lucian, the pain of ordinary people is almost flavorless to me.
Well, there's another one over there... She seems to be that little Zalak's friend.
At first I only took notice of that girl who seemed so similar to Lucian, the lead actress that man chose for his latest play. Yet I didn't expect your ties to make you even tastier than her.
Miss.Christine shakes her tails, her eyes moving between shadows on the ground until they lock onto Tippi's silhouette...
The lady's twin tails twist into strange shapes, as if a woman is wiping her knife and fork before diving into her meal.
(Shakes tails)
(Licks paws after a meal)
...I ought to gift you a small reward.
The lady glances at the crowd rushing towards the Liberi.
A little exercise to aid digestion after her meal, she thinks.
...Why... am I here...?
The Catastrophe's about to hit us, so we came down here to hide from it... But what's with the wrench in my hand?
*thunk*—
Weapons fall from their hands in succession, as the resulting harsh clangs wake even more from their delirium.
They exchange looks of confusion, not knowing why they were all holding weapons, though they do know that they should leave.
Meow—
Miss.Christine...? What're you doing here?
How come they're leaving?
When they were rushing towards me, I suddenly felt my whole body go cold, and lots of different feelings woke up in me at the same time. Like they were fighting...
But then a breeze blew by, I heard their weapons drop, and my body thawed out too.
Like some kinda irresistible force pulled something out of me...
Miss.Christine tilts her head.
She taps her tails twice on the floor, successfully attracting the girl's attention, then points in a direction.
...They are right in front of the control cabin.
*cough* *cough* This place is older than I thought it'd be...
Wow, the devices here are all previous gen stuff...
But that's good news for us, since they're all sealed off to prevent dust and corrosion, and there's no smart locks on anything...
Even I'd find it pretty tough to learn to hack on the spot.
Lemme see...
The storm's moving faster than the plate, so just reversing's a no-go. Turning—turning's the best way out.

But trying to change direction when the plate's already so close to the Catastrophe... No, I can't worry about that!
Where's the backup navigational system...
Here it is!
First open the lock... Great, I'm in.
Then...
Tippi takes a deep breath, and hopes she remembers the steps right.
Pull the lever all the way down!
Bang—
Tippi is thrown against a wall by a huge force. The entire engine layer shakes, with all sorts of strange mechanical noises ringing throughout the tiny space—
That was the plate's 'waist' jerking around, with every segment of the treads below now trying to respond to the change.
The steel giant under her feet is now fighting its own immense and unstoppable inertia by steering sideways, trying to avoid the storm right up ahead—
Tippi feels her teeth ache.
She does not know if it will manage to turn in time, or how long it will take.
All she can do is pray.
Even a little shift will do. We'll have a tiny, tiny chance as long as we don't hit the storm's core.
It has to work, it HAS to work!
I have to... see her one more time.
Meow—
Miss.Christine shakes her tails, jumping in front of the little Liberi to smell the changes in her mood.
A brief relief after achieving her goal, along with confusion and pain from a friend's betrayal... But why is there also a hint of suppressed madness?
No. That smell does not belong to the girl.
Miss.Christine jerks her head up, looking towards a place she cannot see. A companion who has been with her the whole time.
Lucian feels sympathy towards the other person, which means he is currently experiencing twice the pain.
Miss.Christine is somewhat displeased.
She cannot help but recall the night when the Crimson Troupe was destroyed, as Lucian sang under name Blood Diamond—
It is no good omen.
How are you so calm, Lucian?
Are you disappointed? Unmoved by what I've done?
A mannequin pounces on him. While it is an inanimate object, Lucian feels a strong depressive aura that suddenly turns into elation and ecstasy...
As Lucian touches the mannequin, the emotions turn into energy, forcing him backwards.
...Dalton.
There is no doubt these are the feelings Dalton had right before his final rehearsal.
Why don't you fight back? Do you have that little interest in me, babe?
But ever since you played the part of the Messenger and spoke those lines about fate, I haven't been able to take my eyes off you!
...Michael.
The mannequin passes on Moira's emotions and she turns into Michael. The excessive yet artificial man who always wore his heart on his sleeve.
What kind of emotions are you hiding inside yourself?
You let me feel fear, and danger, so much danger...
...Shelly.
Shelly. In the final moments of her life, the neurotic coordinator was completely wrecked by her own fear.
Turning emotions into tangible energy, and any object that passes through your hands becomes a medium to release it...
...So these are your Originium Arts.
I see why he chose you. Why he led you to become what you are now.
A prop master, strings, puppets... all images born just for the stage. When you forget your self, a person becomes a play.
Come, join in our performance, Lucian.
If you're right, and that man roused my abilities, then as his real student, you will make the best dance partner!
Moira laughs and cries loudly... a thousand expressions flash across her face, filling the prop room with a suffocating energy—
There is nowhere for Lucian to run.
Fine.
What?
Moira, I will sing for you... as you wish.
The Blood Diamond's song is a kind of misfortune, for both himself and the audience.
It will rip my throat apart, sending pain straight into your ears... And you will either die, or spend the rest of your life with its melody reverberating in your mind.
If you willingly ignore the truth in your hands, if you're satisfied by such falsehoods, if you can only approach him by destroying yourself...
Then allow me to relieve your pain, and enter the stage once more.
Moira hears a song.
The emotions within flood into her, overwhelming even the song itself. She does not understand what Lucian is singing about, she only feels...
Joy, fear, doubt, rage, hatred, and sorrow—a hundred times stronger than her own...
Before she is completely washed away by the emotions, she cannot help but think—
What sort of experiences must one go through to understand such revelations—or was he simply forced to learn it all within the despair of having nothing at all—
Troupe Leader, Whiteflower is still waiting for us for the curtain call. Why have we come here?
This is just a river... There's nothing here this time of night...
I'm afraid Whiteflower will be upset by tonight's sparse audience.
For this river here is the final stage.
Tell them, Playwright. What does the revised ending look like?

The 'magician's assistant' and the 'lucky audience member' who both died on stage are stuffed into prop boxes and thrown into the river. Once the dust settles, they will wake up and emerge from the river under the stars...
They used their story deaths to distance themselves from their prison of nightmares.
That's... Blood Diamond and Abyss?
Wait, didn't they return to the castle already? Why would they be here...
Are they... running away?
It seems our travel plans have been interrupted, Abyss.
What... how?
The 'magician's assistant' and 'lucky audience member' came back to life... now this is true magic.
With this crowd looking on, it would seem he's a fan of this new improvised ending.
Let's go back, he's waiting for us.
Go back?
We can still run, Lucian.
Come with me. The river's wide enough, and he's far enough away...
If you choose to flee now, he'll let you go. But fear will seep into your bones, and torment you for the rest of your life.
I'll go back to him, learn all his skills, uncover all his secrets, and then destroy it all.
For such is my wretched fate.
Fate? Are you sure it's not just proof that you've become entranced with the Troupe, with him, once more?
If this is the pain and despair he has given me...
Then I will become his fate.
Somehow, you always understand him and his twisted aesthetics.
Yet you never listen to me... or maybe you never really wanted to escape. You've spent far too long trapped in this nightmare.
Fine then. I hope the Crimson Troupe's Blood Diamond will grant a former 'friend' one last favor—
Don't chase me. Don't come after me.
Let us part here, friend... We will meet again once this all falls to ruin.
At some point, the black creature appears inside the prop room, nimbly avoiding Moira's body as she goes over to Lucian.
With his knees bent halfway, and surrounded by mannequin parts scattered all over the ground, for once, Lucian doesn't perform the gesture of a proper gentleman's salute to her.
The lady is even more displeased by his actions.
You haven't sung in a long time, Lucian.
Apologies, Miss.Christine. Did you hurry here as soon as you could?
(Flicks tails without comment)
What a terrible scent. This Zalak compelled you to use your Arts, as well as the skills you were so determined to abandon.
You do know what sort of situation you've put yourself into, yes?
Chaotic, disorderly, uncontrollable... like a ball of unruly yarn I've yet to sort out.
And you wish to face him, even end him, as you are now?
...That is why I've come here, Miss.Christine.
When I stood up from the river as Shalem continued to sink below, my mind was already made.
This is the performance I've repeated the most. I cannot refuse, nor do I want to... not until the true end comes.
Fine.
Perhaps you will become a monster.
Listen, Lucian.
Lucian stays still, and does not look up.
The lady sighs, and her azure eyes light up like never before. She shakes her tails, and her body splits into two identical figures...
One walks into Lucian's shadow, while the other remains sitting before him, staring...
Both Miss.Christines speak in a staggered pattern, like a strange duet—this way she ensures Lucian will hear every word she says.
To be honest, I much prefer 'Phantom'...
It implies nothing good, but it is a name you came up with yourself.
Humans are so troublesome. Sometimes I miss the cafe where we met. Your emotions were much more complex and slovenly than they are now...
But still far better than today... The bitterness of your obsession is making me want to sneeze.
Go, meet him if you must.
If you turn into a madman, I will devour all your emotions... No matter how hard they may be to swallow.
If you cease to exist, I will haunt him, and figure out a way to make his life miserable...
After all, he can't do a thing to me.
Break a leg, Phantom.
Mrow~
Hurry... hurry! Lemme into the underground passage!
Hey! Did the storm blast your eyes blind? Do you have any idea how long I've been in this queue? Why, I oughta teach you a lesson—
No pushing or shoving, people! The passage can only fit two at a time, so don't clog up the entrance and leave space for those behind you! There's still time to get everyone in!
Miss Stone, the plate's western paths and outer guardrails have been destroyed by the storm.
If this keeps up, we won't be able to hold out for long even in the engine layer.

I sent people to move supplies in. If we can survive this calamity...
No, I'm sure we'll make it through.
Miss Rutherford, you should hurry inside! The storm will tear your dress into shreds!
So this is the wind she felt...
Huh? What'd you say?
Nothing.
Didn't you go looking for Tip to discuss the backdrop earlier? Where is she?
She seemed to have figured something out and took off. I couldn't catch up to her.
And we're supposed to be the adults. In the face of a Catastrophe, not one of us is as cool-headed and brave as her.
Because she doesn't know what fear is yet.
Only by experiencing it, hoping through it, and getting crushed by it, will she understand just how idealistic it is to keep going.
Is that not the difficulty and charm of film production itself?
...Don't tell me you wanna finish shooting this damned movie even now?
Miss Stone! The supplies are all underground now, and most of the crew too.
Thank you, Hautin. You should hurry down yourself.
Is there anything else?
I asked around, and no one's seen the director ever since we started approaching the storm.
A stagehand in charge of the west side told me earlier that the Catastrophe destroyed not just the guardrails, but some buildings too...
That's where Steven's studio is!
The storm showers Originium dust all over the exquisite, dream-creating backdrops. In an instant, only the blackened skeletons of a dozen sets remain, slanted supports scattered all over the plate.
Like a cage crushed into pieces.
Lucian pays closer attention to the wind, and it brings more voices—
Shouts and sobs, scolding and cursing, sincere prayers and subconscious wailing...
The hands of fate and the play staged by nature will not allow everyone time to take their positions.
Lucian walks into the ruins—
He sees a figure that absolutely could not have showed up right here, right now.
The Catastrophe shows no respect for the dreams crafted by man, and the storm has removed Lady Dupont's manor from the scene, leaving only a grand staircase in its wake.
Thick clouds lie low, but stage lights still shine onto the steps from afar, turning the ruins into a stage...
A middle-aged man slowly walks up the stairs.
He tugs on a curtain torn apart by the storm, trying to restore its neatness and beauty. Finally, his efforts pay off as the dirt and dust fall off the cloth, revealing its original shade of red.
He grips a bottle in hand as usual, but is not at all a listless drunkard. He checks the stage right before the show begins—there is no one more dedicated to the work than him.
Your habits haven't changed... do you require assistance?
It's almost time, Lucian.
You came at the perfect moment.
I know.
Not surprised?
Should I be?
Of course you wouldn't.

There is no way for a character who has bowed out with a real death to return to the stage.
But this is your play, where all sorts of 'miracles' occur.
'This is where the show begins, and where it should end as well.'
The Playwright might remark on how ingenuously this play has been handled.
Or perhaps he would muse on how perfect it all is.
Because once you push aside all the theatricality, this is what I look like when 'officially collaborating' with you again, after so many years apart.
How is Abyss doing? Oh, my apologies, you are more inclined to call him Shalem, I believe?
Traveling, gathering inspiration, disaster relief... In short, he won't be showing up here.
Of course. The Crimson Troupe has long since ceased to exist, and I'm glad to see the both of you walk out from its haze.
A new role, a new life, a new home... I see the company known as Rhodes Island has provided you many a revelation.
But you? Why have you come here, child?
Should I not have?
No, no. You are our most important individual now, as the curtains are about to fall on this new play.
...I'm already on the stage, after all.
Of course, of course.
The marvelous scene we saw at the opening of this show was all thanks to you and him.
Dalton personally handed you the weapon that would kill him, and his death raised the curtains on this story.
And you witnessed it all through his eyes.
Lucian, surely you felt something was off the moment you held the knife.
While I was relieved to confirm it...
I am also curious: why exactly did you swing that blade with zero hesitation...?
Once an actor takes the stage, anything unexpected must become a part of the performance.
Your lessons have been engraved into my mind, and nothing more—
Troupe Leader.
Long time no see, Lucian.
My deepest apologies for ruining the play.
If you are referring to how you dealt with that Moira girl... then you owe me no apologies, Lucian.
With your intellect, I can only assume you've figured out the original ending of the story.
An old case from five years ago, and a Catastrophe that arrives right on cue. Both the storm and the delayed judgment rain down on this plate.

The Zalak, who once stood outside the story, slowly walks to the center of the stage—and kills all those burdened with sin.
Later, she comes to realize that everything she's done has been driven by delusion. She awakens—hollowed out, broken—and judges the last remaining soul—
Herself.
And you?

In the script, you are the Messenger who happens to pass through, the insignificant newcomer to the cast. You are so far removed from the core conflict, yet in the end, you become the one who ties all the threads together.
The more unexpected the 'coincidence,' the more shocking it is when the 'inevitable' unfolds... This is the allure of drama, something you and I have experienced countless times.
So, you haven't ruined a thing. You've merely added to the performance.
...Just like what you've done for the Troupe.
I've suffered all manner of tragedy in my quest to find you.
Haha. Naturally.
The Troupe's mark at the auction was insufficient proof of my existence, I suppose?
Thankfully.
After all these years, 'death', or rather, 'destruction', remains the plot device you wield so well, the ending you so cherish.
Child, oh child, how I wish you could appreciate its beauty.
Hmm... do I?
I wouldn't say I 'appreciate' it, but I am very familiar with it.
I've acted in so many plays. I've killed so many people. My hands are drenched with blood.
That is the education and training I received in my childhood, the life you instilled in me.
You are my best student, Lucian.
Before the climactic finale arrives, let us review the performance so far. You and I know this routine all too well.
The Troupe Leader smiles and bows towards Lucian. His scepter, wrapped by his tail, points toward the approaching Catastrophe clouds, like a pen dipped in ink.
A familiar scene unfolds before Lucian's eyes. He knows he can't refuse—no, this is what he came for.
Lucian gives a slight nod.
First we have Dalton.
Originally, this middle-aged actor who resembled nothing more than a walking corpse didn't feel this scene was all that significant.

He offered up what little lucidity he could muster, summoned the last dregs of his acting instincts, recited his few remaining lines, and held out until the director called cut—all to earn just enough for another night of drinking.
He has never mourned the actress who died because of him, nor reminisced about his once spirited past. He was not even ashamed of his current sorry state.
You manipulated him.
Indeed. Without my intervention, he would never have taken it upon himself to retrieve the swapped knife and deliver it to you.
But I never commanded him to put his chest up to the blade.
By the time you were acting opposite him, he was already fully awake, fully aware of the danger he was in.
Lucian, your talent shines just as brightly in film as it did on the stage.
Your performance awakened his memories, reminding him of how great an actor he once was, of a time when he was immersed in his roles, expressing real emotions: joy, sorrow, fury, and mirth.
In that moment, 'the Messenger' slipped fully into character, while the looming threat of financial ruin warped 'Sir Dupont's' thoughts.
He had to cover up the truth. He had to do whatever it took to kill the person before him—without pausing to think that his own blade might be turned on him.
You were right there with him—you know better than anyone just how intense his fear and anger were in that moment.
As a farewell performance, it was nearly perfect.
Indeed, death's imminent approach did not frighten him. Instead, it ignited his competitive fire, which had long been dulled by drink. He performed with unrestrained zeal. It was exhilarating!
Then there was Michael and Marion.
The philandering actor and the shrewd costume designer were found hanging above their own set, their farcical romance laid bare for all to see.
Their union was both shameful and absurd—a mess of indulgent desire, scheming ambition, mutual disdain, and mutual exploitation.
There wasn't a trace of anything real about them, inside or out. That Michael—even his ears and tail were fake.
Their deaths served no purpose, save for giving that camera assistant a juicy bit of gossip to peddle.
But you, Lucian, you were at the scene. Even the cat could taste the lingering emotions left behind.
When Moira knocked on that door, when the footsteps of death drew near, in that fleeting moment where there was no time to think, I'm sure you know what each of them did.
Tear stains.
What a fascinating detail.
Tear stains lingered in the corners of his eyes—none of the three Rockefeller brothers had ever shown Lady Dupont such heartfelt emotion.
That fireman's axe was originally meant for Marion.
Yet Michael shielded her from harm, exposing himself to the blade instead.
In the last moments of their lives, that man and that woman, who up to that point had been attacking each other, instinctively protected each other.
They confirmed their true feelings for one another.
Although this, by itself, is not worthy of praise.
You're correct, my child.
However, the audience had already taken their seats. What did they expect to see in this illusion of light and sound?

Ultimately, the schemers will end up with nothing while the hedonists are devoured by their own desires, yes? Naturally. It's the neatest kind of story—just an excuse to trot out well-worn platitudes.
They would never imagine that a flawed, 'unworthy' love could still be real, and still turn heads.
Just as those two never imagined there would be a moment when they grew feelings for each other.
Made possible by death?
Made possible by death.
And then, of course, there was Moira—the one I had the highest hopes for, before you showed up.
Moira rebuilt her faith through another's death.

Every detail about Laura was etched into her mind: her name, her eating habits, her unmistakable smile, her extraordinary life, her violent end, and the killers who still hadn't been brought to justice.
Grief, anger, doubt, hatred—every emotion that rose within her set fire to a buried past, and to herself.
She directed this entire performance.
Why do you insist on downplaying your own role in all of this?
It was you who inserted that ad-libbing, forcing a past that did not originally exist into her mind—bringing 'Laura' into her life.
Yes. And what of it?
Look at her—
She is dancing.
You were her dance partner back then. You must have felt it.
She isn't like the children from the Crimson Troupe, who've been receiving Whiteflower's training since they were young. But when you look at her dancing, her physique, her tears, her smile...
She is acting out a version of life. Or perhaps crafting a kind of truth. Or perhaps she no longer has any constraints, and can be whoever she wants to be.
She will dance through countless people's lives, dithyramb unending, until her mind and body can no longer bear the weight of those emotions.
Or maybe she will finally encounter you, or some other fool seeking the truth, and one night finally awaken from her dream and end her own life.
Regardless of her ultimate fate, her soul will be rich and fulfilled.
Regardless of her ultimate fate, she is already no longer just a nameless prop-maker from the frontier. No longer a Zalak with nothing and no one to hold on to.
You're as talkative as ever, Troupe Leader.
It's been a long time since you've spoken about someone with such passion.
...Not since you, Lucian.
I don't deny that I've exerted my influence on Moira. I encouraged her, just as I once encouraged you.
My child, have you forgotten?
If not for your thirst to perform, you would have died long ago.
When that Catastrophe drove you from your home, when you lost your parents, when you found yourself adrift in that towering castle amongst strangers, you said—
Only when I'm in character, on the stage...
That's the only time my loneliness disappears...
The only time I feel like I belong.
You clung to that feeling, and it kept you alive.
You and Moira... You're both natural-born actors. You both create sorrow and joy from nothing, and use those emotions to survive.
No one can ignore your brilliance!
And for both of you, my two children, death shall be your teacher.
Do you still remember when you hit that wall in your development? When you could not draw inspiration from anything, and were unable to power your performance?
That was when you thrust that Originium into your throat without hesitation.
Then you felt the grip of death, of your inescapable fate!
It gave you fear, jealousy, sorrow, anger, shame, and greed—emotions once foreign to you... And after witnessing all of these things, your heart has become a mirror.
It fueled your desire to dismantle authority, your thirst for destruction. It gave you an endless night—and a song that never ceases.
It turned the unassuming Lucian into a gleaming Blood Diamond.
'Blood Diamond'...
My eternal moniker.
Think of Dalton, child, think of Michael and Marion, think of Laura and Moira. Look at all these souls still struggling through the Catastrophe... and look at yourself.
You're right—death is still the device I wield best, and the ending I've always favored.
But I do not see death as 'destruction'.
It drives the mighty to disgrace themselves, the devoted to break their commandments, the pure to sink into filth, and the heroic to commit treachery!
It gives strength to the dejected, compels the silent to shout their ideals, draws real emotion from the self-absorbed, and frees the timid from their inhibitions!
It shows climbers the next foothold, and gives the farsighted the courage to leap. It turns self-proclaimed beacons into the real thing!
It sows meaning and waters flaws. It cultivates truth and fertilizes falsehoods. It turns dying flesh into blooming flowers, and engraves what would fade without a trace!
It is agony without relief, and overflowing inspiration!
It is drama's cause and consequence, its flesh and its soul—the great force behind creation, the law and tether that binds every ideology, every style, every exploration.
It is the appointed end, where all things return to dust!
The Troupe Leader turns to Lucian, that same smile still lingering on his lips. The wind and sand howl around them, but he remains unfazed.
Child, death is also our chance to reunite.
What a shame. Quite a monologue, yet such little applause.
Because I am the only one standing across from you—and the only one who ever could.
Noise sounds out from all directions, and Lucian listens intently.
The Catastrophe rages. Miss.Christine lets out a low howl—something she would normally never do. Their newly acquainted companions scatter in panic, crying out for help in vain...
As the Troupe Leader said, death is unfolding on stage—what began with a few intense scenes has now taken over the entire production.
You say death is our chance to reunite. I completely agree.
So I won't waste this opportunity.
If I may, I'd like to share a few thoughts of my own, now that you've finished your analysis. After all, both you and I know this routine by heart.
The storm snaps off part of the scaffold, sending a shower of sparks. A nearby rug catches fire, and the flames leap dangerously close to Lucian's side.
But he does not even flinch. He stares intently at the person standing before him and removes his hat.
All this time, ever since you reappeared, you haven't even mentioned the many times I've betrayed you. Why?
(Smiles)
Because that is unimportant. Because you are still you, child.

Getting lost in madness, then returning to clarity. Leaving, only to later return... After all the trials and tribulations, a hard-won reunion is all the more precious. In the end, time can forgive all grievances.
Is that so?

I used to think the Crimson Troupe was your quill, your canvas, and your trap. I thought that in this chaotic world, your audiences sought solace in your performances. I thought you were omnipresent and that no one could escape your grasp.
Which is why the plan that I, Shalem, and the Playwright came up with failed.
Back then, I was young—naive and full of pride. We believed we had seen the Troupe for what it really was. But our carefully planned escape turned out to be just another act in your grand play.
In the end, Shalem fled from you in fear, while I came to you.
You returned to my side.
That was the moment when you decided to learn all of my techniques. You resolved to learn everything you could about the Troupe, then use that information to destroy it.
Your plan was to use death to stop death and to use destruction to stop destruction.
Whiteflower, Bladedance, Shadow... the teachers you once learned from all died by your hands.
...As did I.
I remember my voice, wild and cracking with rage... and the sudden warmth that surged from your chest the moment the blade went in. I thought I had destroyed the Crimson Troupe for good.
And despite all that, I still failed.
You didn't die.
Because death is ever-present, and drama is eternal.
Eternal, yes, but after that, I devoted even more time to studying all your greatest works... including myself.
I wore down your eternity, as any slow-witted disciple might.
And at last, I've arrived, once more stepping into your proud creation: a theater of death, of tragedy, of art.
Now, at last, the Catastrophe has finished setting our final stage.
Troupe Leader, my dear teacher, or perhaps I should call you 'Tragodia'.
I am going to destroy everything that is yours.
Lucian unfastens the inhibitor at his throat. The exposed Originium crystals gleam, as if already rejoicing at the song to come.
He offers the Troupe Leader a deep bow, as the clouds behind him come crashing down.
The flickering lights of the ancient castle, the bloodied corpses of the teachers, the hollow wind in the darkness... Forgetting all of that was not difficult.
Until one day, when Lucian—who has by this time become an assassin—had a dream.
He's still in the old castle, still in that empty hall, sitting alone, singing under the lights, with all the former members of his troupe standing behind him—
As alive as ever.
Child, we cannot truly erase our pasts.
There will always be traces of them.
Do you think I'm scared?
No. I've simply finally confirmed that I've yet to fully tear out the ad-libbing you inserted into my life. In other words, you are still here.
But you're correct: we cannot truly erase our pasts, and there will always be traces of them.
So, to bring your story to an end, I've been walking the same path you once did, for all this time...
I traveled across the entire land!
Calais was a Gaulish border city.

When the allied nations suddenly turned on them, Calais was thrown under siege. Yet thanks to its powerful cannons—and the general hailed as the 'Imperial Standard-Bearer'—Calais held fast for months.
The general stood atop the wall—refusing sleep, refusing rest, refusing to give ground. With their banner still flying high, even the weariest soldiers held the line.
Until the unthinkable happened, the general's most trusted lieutenant opened the gates, a clear act of betrayal.
The Emperor of Gaul had given up on Calais, as by then its fate was sealed.

The Imperial Standard-Bearer had refused to surrender out of honor, and the trusted lieutenant believed he ought to stand by his general. After all, they had already braved so many life-or-death situations together over the decades.
But he knew that holding the line would only lead to more bloodshed—that the city was doomed to become a graveyard.
Perhaps that battle-worn soldier simply realized that although there was no perfect choice, there was a way that would allow him to atone for his sins.
He persuaded the general, who had long since become exhausted, to take a ten minute break. Then he seized his opportunity to open the gate.
Before the allied forces could enter the city, he drove his sword through his own heart... That traitor ended up being the most tragic casualty of the war.
But that is ancient history now, Lucian—so ancient that many of the details have long been lost to the river of time.
Victoria, Leithanien... I spent months traveling between more than a dozen cities across different nations, and at last, I found an aging Vanguard on the brink of death.
The guard had seen the towering flames of Calais with his own eyes. He had found a badge on the traitor's corpse—two faces turned away from each other, one smiling, the other weeping.
We all know what that symbolizes, dear teacher.
Haha.
Then I suppose you made other discoveries as well?
Even earlier in Laterano...
Over 200 years ago, a brilliant cardinal was struck down by gun-knights in the plaza in front of the Basilica.
He was intelligent and compassionate, known as 'the Sankta closest to the Lord.' At just thirty years old, he was already widely regarded as the Pope's heir apparent.
But then, he changed. He grew radical, unyielding, unwieldy. He started preaching, rallying followers, and reaching out to foreign envoys, all in an attempt to check the Pope's authority.

...Until the situation deteriorated into a religious coup that threatened the entire city-state. The Pope was critically wounded, and the gun-knights were deployed. The would-be usurper became the only cardinal in the history of Laterano to die during a coup.
No one knows what exactly happened to him... At some point, in one of the Ecclesia Requietum, he had a chance encounter with a passing Feline—but of course, that is hardly a lead worth chasing.
It must have taken some effort for you to uncover all of this.
Indeed, it was quite troublesome.
I merely offered that Sankta a bit of 'truth'—he was already terminally ill, you know.

Unlike the incompetent Pope, who knew only how to interpret scriptures, his own zealousness knew no bounds. But with limited time, he might not have been able to put his ideals into practice, to hear the church bells ring in the new era.
But in truth, the moment the cardinal struck down the Pope, he came to his senses. He realized that death had never caught up with him after all.
His sudden clarity only plunged him deeper into madness. Faced with the ruin he had wrought, did he choose to walk willingly into the gun-knights' sights?
A microcosm of political tragedy, from which many of the Troupe's plays took root.
Quite a pity, isn't it?
Iberia.

It was during the glorious Golden Age... The lighthouses lit up an approaching ship—only for people to discover it carried nothing but whispering winds from the ocean depths. Ticket stubs stamped with the image of the twin faces were scattered across the deck, and they drifted along with the ship in silence.
The lost sailors sing—
Incite a young fowl, to pick the fruit of pain.
Amidst the spires of Leithanien, ancient Arts recorded the nobles' laments, and desperate cries.
Exile an infant, to arrive at joyful death.
An encampment covered in thick snow.
Weeds walk their own path.
The city in the depths of the sands.
Through tears, falsehoods, and riddles.
He leaves the bed of withered flowers.
...You are everywhere.
