I followed the path you once walked. I crossed the lands, encountering all manner of people and unearthing all manner of secrets—until at last, buried in ancient tomes and tangled rumors, I uncovered your name.
'Tragodia'.
However, the legendary Tragodia is a god, beloved by the people.

He wears a crown of grapevines. He is both intoxicated and intoxicating. He bestows joy and grace. He greets the farmers as they bring in their yearly harvest, guiding them from wilderness to civilization through revelry and celebration.
But the true god of wine is a deceitful caster, a performer, an ascetic, a schemer, a firebrand—a phantom drifting through the currents of history...
A long-lived that defies definition.
(Claps)
Your perseverance astonishes me, child. Even more so than the painstaking devotion you once showed in your performances.
And yet, when you look back at the path I've taken, shouldn't that be enough to affirm the purpose I've been chasing?

As human affairs faded into obscurity, I found—and built—what I came to see as the ultimate vessel for beauty: the theater. I've lived long enough to see the very concept of 'theater' become obsolete.

At last, after tasting both joy and sorrow, I came to understand the precise conditions under which the most fervent emotions bloom. I founded the Troupe—through which I've taught this knowledge to you.
Oh really?
That contradicts what I've learned from my research. Hasn't death always walked by your side?
That grand theory of yours—about death and destruction—was never the result of hard-won insight. You simply believed in it and saw beauty in it, and so did theater.
Let's set aside, for the moment, the brilliance or depravity of human nature that emerges in times of desperation—and turn to some more fundamental questions.
From Laterano to Gaul, from Iberia to Sargon, from the old castle to this Catastrophe-stricken land—you've directed so many performances that shook the soul...
So why have you allowed them to be lost to history?
You don't want them to be remembered?
You exalt the irrational and the unpredictable—so why must every story have the same ending: ruin?
Well, what do you think the answer is, child?
Tell me.
Because it makes everything and everyone unverifiable—both within the theater and beyond.
Because it grants you authority, lets you justify yourself, and turns your so-called 'beauty' into the only possible resolution?!
Steven...
Steven?!
If you're still alive, say something!
...G-Greta?
You really did lock yourself up in your office!
What... what happened outside?
It's too much to explain right now. In short, the entire plate is on a collision course with the Catastrophe, and everyone else has already evacuated to the power deck... Are you alright?
Y-Yeah, something's wrong... The ceiling came down on my leg—I can't move... Ngh—
I'll rescue you!
Dammit! That broken column has sealed the door shut!
It's really heavy! Just wait a moment! Stay with me! Keep talking!
Miss Stone, only now do I understand that I am in no way, shape, or form a genius.
Huh?
That 'god' came to see me.
He hasn't abandoned me. He's shown me a new way of thinking. Actually, he's always been teaching me.
I like to think I make good use of my characters' deaths—of every desperate situation—to nourish my stories. I did try that, after all, in The Death of Lady Dupont. Why else would it be called that?
Laura from five years ago, and now Dalton, Shelly, Michael, and Marion... Each of their deaths was steeped in drama: vivid, poignant, and all too real.
Those fragments are like autumn leaves—crimson as blood, and scattered everywhere. Each one is enough to stain the blank canvas of my mind with vivid color.
But still—I can't write an ending that satisfies me! I still haven't figured out what kind of scene Lady Dupont's death ought to be!
If death and destruction are the way of the god of theater—if they're the skills a true genius of the stage must master—well, I mean, I... I've never been able to learn them.
I'm just a *cough* worthless hack...
For the love of Borgnine! How can you be so stupid?!
...Huh?
Steven, the Script Auctions are anonymous. You know that, right?
Dalton, Abner—everyone in the crew has been shaped by what happened five years ago. I brought them together on purpose... But you, you were the one exception.
When I approved the outline for the script, I had no idea it was written by you.

I simply felt, from the bottom of my heart, that the story of Lady Dupont—a woman trapped by her brother's lies and need for control, and tormented by the desires of three so-called suitors—was worth my vote of approval.
I knew she'd be a great protagonist for an amazing story, whether she lived or died.
I have never doubted my own vision.
Hey, say something! What are you getting all melodramatic for while I'm out here hauling rocks?!
So it turns out I just don't agree with...
What are you saying?
Ugh! Saget! So it turns out I just don't agree with the 'god's' methods.
There we are! I did it!
This place is about to collapse! I'll help you! Come on!
Wait, wait, wait, Miss Stone.
What now?
Greta helps hold Steven steady as they both come to a stop, and he looks to the side.
There is a wall, only half of it still standing. Faint remnants of vivid graffiti stand out harshly against the grimy, dim surroundings.
It is the giant promotional mural Tippi had painted for the crew, and by some stroke of luck, the title card remains unscathed amidst all the wind and flying debris—
The Death of Lady Dupont.
Steven raises a bloodstained arm and, with considerable effort, wipes away the first three words.
My God...
...It's beautiful.

Not even the most imaginative set designer in Wrankwood could have created a scene like this. Who's to say that ending a film shoot under the shadow of a Catastrophe isn't, in fact, the most fitting conclusion of all?
This isn't just a publicity stunt. I'm finding myself more and more impressed by Greta's resolve.
Alright, that's enough of that. Let's get back to the power deck. It's way too dangerous out here!
Hey, Melanie, leading lady, where are you going? Have you gone mad?!
Abner, you—all of you—never really believed this 'leading lady' could make a comeback with this film, did you?
You don't even think we'll finish filming it...
Honestly, I feel the same. I mean, we don't even have the ending figured out yet, and this whole plate is about to collapse.
But that doesn't mean you have to die for it, does it?
The Catastrophe is closing in.
The howling wind is nature's roar, its fury written across the face of the clouds.
But Melanie still walks forward to face it.
Who said I have a death wish?
Who says Lady Dupont has a death wish?

Who says Lady Dupont is meant to die under her brother's oppression, felled by the swords of the three Rockefellers, at the hands of those onlookers who sought to curse her, humiliate her, drive her out...
Must death be a symbol?
She should instead, on a night no one could have foreseen, walk straight into a storm just as wild and chaotic as this one, leaving that prison of a castle behind.
She shall not perish, because this storm is the chariot that will lead her to freedom!
You must have realized, Abner, that on my journey I've always regarded Marianne as my imaginary enemy, yes?
...Yeah.
But I know I'll never catch up to her.
She was a true genius—born to inhabit any role she was given. Even fate itself seemed to orchestrate one final Catastrophe just so she could exit this business in a blaze of brilliance.
And I'm nobody special.
An ordinary woman from a small town, who traveled to Wrankwood and worked her way up role by role, film by film, for over a decade. Gaining experience and recognition...

That's why I care so much about seniority, status, and etiquette. The aspects of film set culture that so many scoff at... Because that sort of recognition is the reward I deserve as an ordinary person.
This land is quite cruel to ordinary people, Abner.
Marianne's retirement gave me the chance to step into the spotlight, but it took only a single slip-up for me to lose everything I'd gained along the way.
My acting skills have fallen off a cliff. My reputation is a shambles. I've tried everything I could think of, but I just couldn't seem to get it back...
It's ridiculous! I've never wanted to admit this, but it's true... If Marianne hadn't retired, I'd never have caught up to her!
And right this very moment, this Catastrophe is destroying the entire plate, along with my last hope.
But couldn't you also say fate is smiling upon me?
At least I finally have the courage... to let go of my inhibitions, the courage to...
At least I finally mustered the courage to take this first leap.
Come to me, Lady Dupont. Come with Melanie Rutherford, and dance together with the Catastrophe...
Even if no one is filming us.
What are you still doing here, Abner?
Check your camera. You didn't turn it on, did you?
What, what do you mean?
What if I'd gotten swept away by the storm? What if your camera had accidentally captured my death, just like before?
The last thing I want is to belittle your work, and to force you to once again resent the tool of your trade.
You know, Abner, I've always thought you were dumb.
Hey, what would you know?
That footage you accidentally recorded back then at least stands as irrefutable evidence—ensuring that a girl named Laura wouldn't vanish from this land without a trace.
Although I couldn't say if that's enough meaning for it to meet your exacting standards.
Friedkin Kubrick! I am an idiot!
This 'dream-making machine' was only made in the first place because people wanted more eyes to see the land with.
How long can you hold on, Melanie?
...I hope I don't break this thing.
Let's finish filming!
Can you see them? Can you hear them... those still fighting to survive the Catastrophe?
Nothing ever truly comes from nothing—not even the most wondrous of art. And it is never death that suddenly awakens the lost or steels the timid.
They always had it in them.
So, answer my question, Tragodia.

Are you attempting to remind me that death would simply end this story, this performance? That there would be nary a trace left after the curtains fall? That I will have, objectively speaking, escaped any and all scrutiny and criticism of my work?
That I am a thin-skinned artist?
You really did find an interesting angle, Lucian.
Perhaps what you say is right, but how are you going to change the ending that has already been written?
That child, Tippi, really did adjust the path of the mobile plate, using only techniques she picked up from watching film reels.
But the shift in its trajectory was insufficient. Nearly half the plate remains on a collision course with the storm.
Lucian, you faced a Catastrophe when you were young. You should know that one of that scale will destroy everything on this plate.
Lucian gently strokes the crystal on his throat.
I haven't sung since releasing the inhibitor.
Singing. It is Blood Diamond's song.

The song that once lit up countless stages, before dying out amidst the darkness of that old castle. Today, it echoes once more. The sound passes through cracks in the ruins and the Catastrophe itself, ringing clearly in everyone's ears.
Singing with such total devotion?
It seems that dreams can also be used as barriers. This is the first time I've ever seen you use your Arts in reverse, Lucian.
Are you trying to protect everyone with the voice that once brought pain to yourself and disaster to others?
Are you trying to face this Catastrophe alone?
Alone? No. You're here with me.
Hold your scepter steady, Tragodia.
From the moment I rose from that river and walked back to your side... From the moment I returned to the Troupe and stood before you once again...
I have only ever had one motivation.
At the climax of every tragedy, there is always a fierce, cathartic battle—be it inside a palace, atop a tower... or beneath this ever-shifting sky.
Let us finish this drama, where the student kills the teacher!
Haha! My favorite student! My best actor! Everything that is headed for destruction shall serve as your backdrop. I am so glad you can enjoy this moment!
Even I can't help but be swept away in it! I've forgotten all other matters!
But you should know that killing a long-lived is meaningless.
You just told me that I found an interesting angle.
But it's not interesting, far from it.
You aren't a thin-skinned artist. You've just been left with no other choice.
It is not us who need to die, not the audience, not the common folk, bound by the laws of ordinary reality.
It's you—Tragodia.

You've walked through history. You've founded the Troupe. You've created dreams from darkness. You've fed both me and Shalem with despair. You've drawn in lost souls, one after another, and lashed actor after actor...
You taught us to feel pain, to learn evil, to be stained with blood, to sing of destruction. You taught us to dress for the stage, to wear a mask in life, to believe in what you called rebirth.
You built the story and drew in the audience. You chose that Lateran cardinal, that Gaulish general, Whiteflower, Bladedance, Shadow, Dalton, Shelly, Moira...
You've chosen a million 'Lucians' with which to bring about death.
You exhausted its form, crafted its style, and perfected its meaning—
Because it is all so far away from you.
...So far away.
Long lived—death is the one shore you will never reach. Director—death is the one performance you can never take part in. Tragodia—death is the one art you will never embody!
All your aesthetics, your insights, your experiences—everything you pushed us to pursue has done nothing for you in the end.
I once thought this brought you joy, but your sorrow has always outweighed your sense of accomplishment!
If you yourself are the theater, then your very existence is a paradox!
The ending is nigh, isn't it?
Do you feel resentful or helpless?
Do you find this funny or confusing?
Or have you simply ignored it?
Have you never felt the same pain that I have?
Tragodia is uncharacteristically silent, his eyes fixed on the face in front of him that is lost in emotion.
Lucian shouts loudly. The raging storm does not even distort his voice in the slightest. He sings, and chants—while the Originium crystals creep visibly along his neck.
Does it hurt, child?
Ngh—
Yes. Of course it hurts.
If you keep using your Arts without restraint, it will kill you, child.
Are you jealous?
Hahahaha.
Perhaps, but your performance really is moving.

Lucian responds to Tragodia with even greater fervor. His surging emotions give him newfound strength, and at last, he seizes the upper hand in their clash. In an instant, he plunges the scepter into Tragodia's torso.
Pain is my blade, and at last, I have driven it into the one who caused my pain in the first place—
You!
The curtain must fall on every performance.
Cycles will not help shape our so-called 'destiny'.
Child, I've just gained new inspiration.
This story deserves to be continued.
The plate beneath their feet is swaying and shaking, but is starting to stabilize.
People start to poke their heads out from the ruins and look at the receding disaster. They can hardly believe the Catastrophe they have just experienced.
Hundreds of broken masks drop to the ground, as Tragodia slowly falls to his feet.
Lucian is sure that he has pierced Tragodia's heart and completed his grand, murderous drama. However, he also notices a change in himself.
These clothes, this scepter, this crown...
Have you given me a new ad-libbing?
The silver-white hem of his robe flutters in the wind, brushed by grains of sand, yet spotless as ever. Lucian silently studies the elegant garment—
It is flamboyant and exquisite and fits him perfectly—as if it had been tailored just for him. He could walk into any festival or onto any stage in it, and it would suit him perfectly.
It is as if this is the Catastrophe's gift to him, his reward for slaying his teacher.
The theme of this murder was clearly 'betrayal', yet at the last moment it became a 'passing of the torch'?
Lucian is silent.
The fallen Tragodia has disappeared. Where he once lay, there is only blood, and his mask. The dark red carpet extends upward along the steps, without end.
This is the path he was meant to walk, and it is now free of all obstacles. These thoughts spring to Lucian's mind from nowhere.
So he climbs the stairs without lowering his head or looking back.
Just then, he hears Tragodia's voice.
Lucian, you are the finest actor in the land. You have honed your craft well, and you use your powers of reason to grasp hold of the mask of emotions.
You know well my origins, so it should be clear to you that you have not ended me by merely killing me.
You shouldn't pin your hopes on a scene that was once performed in an ancient castle.
Or is it that you were certain I would think this way—make this choice?
Haha. It's all so interesting.
Maybe you were right. Death is the one shore I will never reach, the one performance I can never take part in, and the one art I can never embody.

lines.st: He leaves the bed of withered flowers.
He writes his epitaph into the stirring dust,

lines.x: 'Death or destruction' is the greatest gift you could ever give your teacher.
But have you considered that what you now reject—and what I've left behind—is only one of theater's countless possibilities, one of the many ways beauty can be defined?
And therefore, that what you've just killed was but one of my thousands of existences?
Go on.
Life rushes by, but the theater endures.
The theater will never perish, and aesthetics will always change, so therefore I will always stand at the pinnacle of everything.
The theater cannot be killed—I can only redefine it, replace one theater with another, overthrow old aesthetics with new?
I can become the new one—the new you?
If that is the only possible fate for the two of us—
Then I accept it.
You see, Lucian? Once again, we've entered into a tacit agreement. Haha!
Continue forward. Climb to the highest point on the stage.
You shall wear the crown of 'performance'. You shall wield the scepter of 'inspiration'. You shall wear the years of performing and being performed.
This process will only add to your pain, but you must accept it.
Child, you shall walk from here with both my blessing and my curse.
I have fulfilled the promise I made to you when we first met: I will never be an obstacle to you... or an obstacle to theater.
This is my final gift to you.
How much further can you walk?
What other answers can you come to—
'Tragodia'.
Lucian does not answer, and the sound finally stops.
He stands at the top of the stairs. The storm is far away now, and blood trails behind him.
He loves this desolate scene, he loves the unformed Arts in his body, and he stretches out all his pain.
Then the new Tragodia sings his song—

lines.loc: Columbia, Wrankwood Outskirts, Federal Nomadic PrisonTwo months later

lines.x: Molly, you've lost so much weight!
I did spend two whole months in bed.
I went looking for you once the plate stopped. When I saw medics carrying you out all covered in blood, and you weren't moving, I thought you were dead...
His singing voice is... terrifying.
But he only wanted to stop me. He didn't want to kill me.
It definitely wasn't a coincidence that he bumped into Greta at the auction, like she said. He had another motive to join the film crew.
And what was that?
He was looking for something that exists, something that's always with us, even though we know nothing about it.

Okay... I knew he was a mysterious dude, but now I'm even more confused.
But forget about him!
I'm sorry for taking so long to come see you. Mostly because they make it a real pain to file a visitation application for this place.
I know, Tippi.
But I used you and caused others to misunderstand you...
Lucian was right. I was ignoring the truth in my hands. In my final moments of madness, I didn't even... think of you...
Listen to me, Molly.
No matter what you went through, no matter what your goal, you hurt the people who were by our side practically twenty-four seven. You're a criminal, and I won't ever forgive you for that!
...But that doesn't mean I can't also miss the times we would crash on the sofa to watch horror flicks, just the two of us.
Wasn't that fun? Wasn't that, like, real?
...Yeah.
When I was stuck in bed, my wounds hurt so bad I couldn't sleep. I kept recalling everything that happened...
A whole night would pass, but only my pain was real.
Honestly, it doesn't matter how absurd a story gets, or if we're all puppets of fate or whatever. It's not like I never had a choice.
If only I'd focused more on the movies we were watching, instead of thinking about the truth I thought was real—hatred, madness, blood, and puppets.
I'm sorry, Tippi.
If I'd met you earlier, maybe you wouldn't have even needed 'Laura'.
The final day of your trial's in two days, right? I talked to a few lawyers, and it seems like...
Don't let my ending bother you.
The warden's nice here. He let me stay in the prison's logistics department during these last few days, to make stuff the prisoners can use.
I'm not living like I was before, merely waiting in vain for the finale.
Good, good.
Haha, you're almost out of visiting time, so let's talk about happier stuff.
What happened to the movie we all shot together?

lines.loc: Columbia, central Wrankwood, 'Avant-Garde Film Festival' venueOne day ago

lines.x: Wow, everyone's here already! Sorry I'm late!
Nervous, Steven?
A bit.
Relax.
We shot that ridiculous finale, and then it went to editing, color grading, and music production... Everyone did their best to complete production in only two months.
I'm glad we agreed on the title change, Steven.
I should be thanking you again for your final performance inside the Catastrophe. Otherwise, deleting those three words would've been nothing but pretentious navel-gazing.
Lady Dupont is by no means as attention-grabbing as The Death of Lady Dupont, but let Greta worry about the marketing... There is no doubt in my mind that our new title suits the story best.

Right, I'll handle it.
I'll get Lady Dupont into theaters...
then visit a police station to do what I have to do.
Pinch me. I'm not seeing things, am I?
Pinch you? Just snap your shutter already! This is guaranteed to go on the front page tomorrow!
How did the niche AGFF attract such a distinguished guest? How much did the organizers spend?
How many years has it been since Marianne Blake showed up in Wrankwood?
Grandma Jara!
It's been a while, Tip.
Judging by your outfit... let me guess what your role in this was. Script supervisor? Prop master? Stuntwoman? Or some kind of 'Furniture City Warrior'?
Background painter, of course!
I've been in this crew for half a year, and was even selected to join the Wrankwood studio plate restoration project last week!
The plate our crew rented got totally wrecked by the Catastrophe, so the boss put me in charge of the design work once it's rebuilt, and I've been, like, soooo busy...
Haha, I see you've finally figured out what you want to do.
I told you before, didn't I? I love movies, every last part of them!
And now, I finally found out what my favorite part is!
So, you don't plan on going back?
Hehe, think you could keep shielding me from the old man?
Tip, your grandfather may be an old friend of mine who used to be my finest partner in the industry, but that doesn't mean he'll let me tell him how to raise his grandkids.

Just tell him this the next time you have tea with him:
Ahem—
'We're all retired, aren't we, old chap? Why waste our time worrying about the little things?'
'I know Tip didn't use any of your contacts, nor did she follow your plan to become a historical film director, but isn't she living it up in Wrankwood?'
'Who knows, half the backdrops in town might be spray-painted by your granddaughter one day!'
Alright, I recorded all that. I'll play it for him the next time we meet.
...Aw, Grandma Jara!
Fine, fine. But I might not have the time to see your grandpa the next time I'm in Columbia.
Oh yeah, didn't you go all the way to Rim Billiton for some fresh air? Did you come back for Rhine Lab stuff?

That's too many questions, Tip.
Once the screening's done, I have to head over to Max D.C. to see the vice president…
So at least let me have a chat with an old friend, will you?
Long time no see, Marianne.
Just call me Jara.
After so many years away from the silver screen, 'Marianne' sounds like a stranger's name to me.
Either way, I have to thank you... Jara.
I'm sure you know that given my current reputation, I don't have much of an influence in the industry anymore.
So without your help, the AGFF would never have allowed Lady Dupont as a late submission.
It was my pleasure.
Though, I must say, I had no idea that Tippi was actually—
I would have helped you with or without her.
I just watched the part with the Catastrophe, and felt it'd be such a pity for the audience to miss out on the actress's performance. So I thought I'd do an old friend a favor.
But even if Lady Dupont gets free marketing at AGFF, that doesn't always translate into box office numbers.
Melanie, you know better than I that if Wrankwood were a person, he would be the most forgetful and least-tolerant person there is.
...It's no guarantee that he'd accept a lover running back into his arms.
Besides, you did destroy an entire plate. Even if the film's a smash hit, you might not make enough to pay for the damages.
At least I did it. And I have no regrets.
But rather than mulling over all that, I'd like to... ask you a question.
Go ahead.
When you encountered a Catastrophe while shooting The Eternally Shining Star, the camera caught you as the storm nearly missed you...
Was it just an accident, like you said at the press conference, or...
Did you also move toward it of your own will?
Is the answer to that still important to you, even now?
Hahahaha. No.
*sigh* It sure is hard to say if I really did beat you in the end!
The splendidly dressed female lead laughs heartily before every camera in the room, though she does not even know what she is laughing at.

After a long time, she opens her arms towards her former rival, the genius she always thought she would never catch up to, the one she had always exchanged polite greetings with on camera, for a hug...
And it is returned in kind.
Let's head in.

lines.st: Art Department Carl, Bob, Tippi, Jericho...

lines.x: ...It's over?
Yup.
Thoughts on the film?
I heard about what happened behind the scenes—the murders, the Catastrophe, even the project management was a mess.
So before I sat down to watch it, I thought the film couldn't possibly live up to all that. But...
An absurd noble's marriage breakdown ending with a Catastrophe—the plot twists made sense, but so many of the characters were just bizarre...
It's hard to say if it's good or bad, but it's a lot to chew on.
Same, to be honest.
Story aside, even the production was pretty controversial. Like, the cinematography is inconsistent at times...

The first part was shot meticulously like a serious historical drama, but it becomes rougher and rougher towards the end, especially the Catastrophe scenes, where it's totally filmed like a documentary!
It's definitely going to be polarizing. The fans will rate it highly, while the haters are going to hate it.
But that's what makes it interesting, isn't it?
Wrankwood's been getting more and more boring over the years, and that goes for AGFF films too...
But this one? I'd buy a ticket to Lady Dupont.
Let's see what the director and cast have to say during the post-screening round table.
Oh yeah, I heard that unlucky-looking gal in the front worked on it too...
Hoo boy... My luck is awful.
After a month of applications, I finally landed a spot as their last stagehand, but then all of that happened the moment I arrived on set. I lost so much sleep after running into those corpses...
And if it weren't for the insomnia, I wouldn't have kicked the wall with my mind all messed up, and broken my leg when I was trying to follow everyone down to safety when the Catastrophe came near...
And if I didn't break my leg, I wouldn't have missed my bus and missed the first half of the screening...
The unlucky stagehand mutters to herself as the credits roll. She recalls her experience and cannot help but feel sorrowful about it all.
Even the images on the screen feel strange to her. The familiar faces and scenes have all been assembled in a way she cannot understand...
Just like her 'topsy-turvy' life.
Then, she spots her name at the end of the credits.
'Stage Department: Hautin'...
No matter how the hand of fate may change, it will eventually leave a line, a truth you believe in.
It's over. Aren't you taking part in the post-screening round table?
I'm not with them. Besides... the two of you are here.
Hah, true enough.
Yes, it was wonderful... Be it the film itself or otherwise.
Lucian...
Hm?
Is he dead for good this time?
Or is he still alive somehow?
I'd have to think about that.
But all you need to know, Shalem, is that you won't have to worry about shadows from the past invading your dreams—even when you're on Rhodes Island—ever again.
And Playwright, you may write your scripts as you wish. About any topic, in any style.
The Crimson Troupe is no more. You two are free.
And you?
You just said you'd have to think about his ending—which means you're stuck on that path, correct?
'Drama never dies'... So is the one sitting beside me right now no longer 'Blood Diamond', but 'Tragodia'?
It sounds like you've paid a heavy price, Lucian.
I wouldn't say so.
If our previous attempts at escape or revenge were just scripts for us to follow, then now, we can finally step into the future.
I will travel again, sing and perform again, until I am certain that what I create manages to match my joy and pain.
It could be a new troupe or a new proposition, new adventures and new stories. Or, a more dazzling tomorrow, a more tragic truth...
Hey, Shalem. We're all buddies here... would you like to lend him a hand?
Lucian, a-are you sure about this?
Of course.

Those who've lost themselves in their haste. The ascetics who've closed off their senses and their selves. The laypeople who trudge through both highs and lows. And the creatures who chew on their pain.
This land has never lacked for actors.
...So I welcome you both to join the 'troupe'.
